questions.question_text: Rate the extent to which maintenance technicians can independently diagnose and repair equipment failures within their assigned asset class without requiring external support.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review completed work orders for rework rates and callbacks within 30 days. Sample 20+ closed corrective work orders.

scoring_rubric.1: Technicians frequently cannot complete basic repairs; external contractors handle >50% of corrective work.
scoring_rubric.2: Technicians handle routine repairs but require supervision or external help for most non-routine failures.
scoring_rubric.3: Technicians independently complete most standard repairs; external help needed only for specialized or OEM-specific issues.
scoring_rubric.4: Technicians independently diagnose and repair complex failures across multiple asset classes with minimal external support (<10%).
scoring_rubric.5: Technicians are recognized subject-matter experts who mentor others, perform root-cause analysis, and contribute to reliability improvement initiatives.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who can independently troubleshoot a variable-frequency drive fault, identify the root cause, replace components, and verify proper operation without escalation scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What percentage of maintenance technicians hold current, relevant trade certifications or licenses for their assigned work areas?
questions.evidence_guidance: Provide certification tracking spreadsheet or HR records showing current certifications vs. required certifications by role.

scoring_rubric.1: Less than 20% of technicians hold required certifications; no tracking system exists.
scoring_rubric.2: 20-40% certified; tracking is informal or incomplete.
scoring_rubric.3: 40-70% certified; formal tracking exists but gaps are not actively managed.
scoring_rubric.4: 70-90% certified; certification gaps are tracked and addressed through a structured development plan.
scoring_rubric.5: >90% certified; proactive recertification program with succession planning for critical skill areas.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 75% of electricians hold current journeyman cards and there is a documented plan to certify the remaining 25% within 12 months scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do technicians demonstrate proficiency in precision maintenance techniques (e.g., laser alignment, precision balancing, proper torquing, contamination control) during observed work execution?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe 3-5 active maintenance jobs. Check for use of torque wrenches, dial indicators, laser alignment tools, and contamination-control practices.

scoring_rubric.1: No evidence of precision maintenance practices; tools are unavailable or unused.
scoring_rubric.2: Some precision tools available but rarely used; techniques applied inconsistently.
scoring_rubric.3: Precision techniques applied on critical assets; standard work procedures reference precision methods.
scoring_rubric.4: Precision maintenance is the norm across most asset classes; technicians are trained and audited on technique.
scoring_rubric.5: Precision maintenance is embedded in culture; technicians self-audit, data confirms reduced vibration/alignment defects, and continuous improvement is evident.

questions.calibration_anchor: Observing a technician using a laser alignment tool with documented tolerances, recording as-found/as-left readings, and flagging soft-foot conditions scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a documented skills matrix that maps each technician's competencies against the technical requirements of the assets they maintain?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request the skills matrix document. Verify it has been updated within the last 12 months and covers all active technicians.

scoring_rubric.1: No skills matrix exists.
scoring_rubric.2: An informal or outdated skills matrix exists but is not actively used for work assignment or development.
scoring_rubric.3: A formal skills matrix exists and is updated annually; used for identifying training needs.
scoring_rubric.4: Skills matrix is integrated with work assignment; supervisors use it to match technicians to tasks and identify gaps.
scoring_rubric.5: Skills matrix is a living document updated in real time, linked to CMMS for skill-based dispatching, and drives succession planning.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a current Excel-based skills matrix that supervisors reference monthly for training decisions but is not linked to CMMS scores a 3.

questions.question_text: How effectively do technicians utilize condition monitoring tools (vibration analyzers, ultrasonic detectors, infrared cameras) assigned to them?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review PdM route completion rates, data quality assessments, and sample condition monitoring reports from the last 90 days.

scoring_rubric.1: Condition monitoring tools are not available or technicians are not trained to use them.
scoring_rubric.2: Tools are available but used sporadically; data collected is often unusable or not analyzed.
scoring_rubric.3: Technicians complete assigned routes; data is collected consistently but analysis is performed by others.
scoring_rubric.4: Technicians collect high-quality data on schedule, can interpret basic results, and escalate anomalies promptly.
scoring_rubric.5: Technicians are certified analysts who collect, interpret, and act on condition data; they contribute to program optimization and technology evaluation.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who completes vibration routes on time, flags a bearing defect frequency on a pump, and writes a corrective work order with urgency context scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What is the first-time fix rate for corrective maintenance work orders completed by in-house technicians over the past 12 months?
questions.evidence_guidance: Extract CMMS data on corrective WOs closed in last 12 months. Calculate percentage where no rework or callback WO was generated within 30 days for the same asset/failure mode.

scoring_rubric.1: First-time fix rate <50% or not measured.
scoring_rubric.2: First-time fix rate 50-65%.
scoring_rubric.3: First-time fix rate 65-80%.
scoring_rubric.4: First-time fix rate 80-92%.
scoring_rubric.5: First-time fix rate >92% with root-cause tracking on all rework events.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site reporting 85% first-time fix rate with a documented process for analyzing rework causes scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do technicians actively participate in root cause analysis (RCA) or failure analysis activities when equipment failures occur?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review RCA reports from the last 12 months. Check for technician participation listed in the team. Interview 2-3 technicians about their RCA involvement.

scoring_rubric.1: Technicians are not involved in any failure analysis activities.
scoring_rubric.2: Technicians occasionally provide verbal input but are not formal participants in RCA.
scoring_rubric.3: Technicians participate in RCA when invited; their input is documented.
scoring_rubric.4: Technicians are expected participants in all RCAs for their asset area; they provide detailed failure observations and recommend corrective actions.
scoring_rubric.5: Technicians lead or co-lead RCA investigations, have been trained in formal RCA methods (5-Why, fishbone, fault tree), and track action item closure.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who attends all RCAs for their area, provides as-found condition photos, and can articulate how the failure progressed scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are multi-craft or cross-functional skills actively developed among maintenance technicians to reduce dependency on single-skill specialists?

scoring_rubric.1: No cross-training exists; technicians are strictly single-craft.
scoring_rubric.2: Some informal cross-training occurs but is not planned or tracked.
scoring_rubric.3: A formal multi-craft development program exists with defined progression paths.
scoring_rubric.4: Most technicians have documented secondary craft skills; work assignment reflects multi-craft capability.
scoring_rubric.5: Multi-craft capability is the standard; technicians rotate across disciplines, reducing crew size requirements and improving response time.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 60% of mechanics have completed electrical safety and basic instrumentation training, and this is tracked in the skills matrix, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Can technicians accurately complete required CMMS work order fields including failure codes, labor hours, parts used, and as-found/as-left conditions?
questions.evidence_guidance: Audit 20 randomly selected closed work orders for completeness. Check for populated failure code, labor hours, parts list, and condition narrative fields.

scoring_rubric.1: Work orders are mostly blank; technicians do not use CMMS for feedback.
scoring_rubric.2: <40% of audited work orders have complete required fields.
scoring_rubric.3: 40-70% of audited work orders are complete; common gaps in failure coding and condition narratives.
scoring_rubric.4: 70-90% of work orders are complete with useful detail; failure codes are applied correctly.
scoring_rubric.5: >90% of work orders are complete, accurate, and provide actionable failure and condition data.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor finding 80% of sampled work orders with correct failure codes, actual labor hours, and a brief condition description scores a 4.

questions.question_text: How frequently do technicians identify and report defects or abnormal conditions outside the scope of their current work order?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review defect identification tags or notifications generated by technicians over the last 90 days. Compare to total PM/CM work orders completed.

scoring_rubric.1: Technicians do not report defects outside their current scope of work.
scoring_rubric.2: Defect reporting occurs occasionally and informally (verbal only).
scoring_rubric.3: A defect tagging system exists; technicians report defects but volume is low (<1 per 10 WOs).
scoring_rubric.4: Defect identification is an expected behavior; technicians report 1-3 defects per 10 WOs with adequate detail for planning.
scoring_rubric.5: Proactive defect identification is cultural; technicians average >3 defects per 10 WOs, defects are triaged weekly, and metrics are tracked.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who, during a pump PM, tags a nearby leaking valve, a corroded pipe support, and a missing guard — and enters all three as notifications in CMMS — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the overall effectiveness of the maintenance training program in developing technician competency and closing identified skill gaps.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the training program plan, annual training hours per technician, and any post-training competency assessments. Compare planned vs. actual training hours.

scoring_rubric.1: No formal maintenance training program exists; training is ad-hoc or non-existent.
scoring_rubric.2: Training is reactive and vendor-driven only; no internal curriculum or skills gap analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: A formal training program exists with an annual plan, but execution is inconsistent and effectiveness is not measured.
scoring_rubric.4: Training is planned, budgeted, and aligned to skills matrix gaps; post-training assessments verify competency.
scoring_rubric.5: Training program is world-class with blended learning, OJT verification, refresher schedules, and direct linkage to performance metrics (rework, MTTR).

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a documented annual training plan that addresses top-5 skill gaps from the skills matrix, delivers 40+ hours per technician, and includes hands-on assessments scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What is the average annual training hours per maintenance technician (including OJT, classroom, e-learning, and vendor training)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Provide training records or LMS reports showing total training hours by technician for the last 12 months.

scoring_rubric.1: Less than 8 hours per technician per year or not tracked.
scoring_rubric.2: 8-20 hours per technician per year; mostly safety-mandated training.
scoring_rubric.3: 20-40 hours per technician per year; mix of safety and technical skills.
scoring_rubric.4: 40-80 hours per technician per year; structured curriculum addressing identified skill gaps.
scoring_rubric.5: >80 hours per technician per year with competency-based progression and verified learning outcomes.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site averaging 50 hours of training per technician with a documented mix of technical, safety, and soft-skills training scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a structured on-the-job training (OJT) program with qualified trainers, task-specific checklists, and sign-off requirements?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request OJT program documentation, trainer qualification records, and sample completed OJT checklists.

scoring_rubric.1: No structured OJT program; new hires learn by shadowing without any documented process.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal OJT exists; new hires shadow experienced technicians but no checklists or sign-offs are used.
scoring_rubric.3: Formal OJT program with task checklists exists; trainers are identified but not formally qualified.
scoring_rubric.4: OJT program has qualified trainers, task-specific checklists, competency verification, and sign-off requirements.
scoring_rubric.5: OJT is fully integrated with the skills matrix, includes progressive task complexity, has formal trainer certification, and outcomes are tracked in the LMS.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where new mechanics complete a 90-day OJT checklist covering 25 critical tasks, signed off by a designated trainer, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are training needs systematically identified based on skills gap analysis, equipment failure trends, and new technology introductions?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the process for identifying training needs. Look for linkage between failure data, skills matrix gaps, and training plan priorities.

scoring_rubric.1: Training needs are not identified systematically; training is ad-hoc.
scoring_rubric.2: Training needs are based on supervisor opinion only; no data-driven analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: Skills matrix gaps drive some training decisions; failure data is occasionally reviewed.
scoring_rubric.4: Training needs are formally identified from skills matrix, failure trends, RCA findings, and new technology requirements; prioritized annually.
scoring_rubric.5: Training needs analysis is a continuous process integrated with reliability data, succession planning, and strategic asset management objectives.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that reviews the top 10 failure modes annually and cross-references them with skills matrix gaps to set training priorities scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization have a formal apprenticeship or journeyman development program for maintenance trades?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request documentation of apprenticeship program structure, current enrollment, completion rates, and progression criteria.

scoring_rubric.1: No apprenticeship or formal development program exists.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal mentoring occurs but there is no structured apprenticeship program.
scoring_rubric.3: A formal apprenticeship program exists but enrollment is limited and completion rates are low.
scoring_rubric.4: Active apprenticeship program with structured curriculum, defined milestones, competency assessments, and >70% completion rate.
scoring_rubric.5: Apprenticeship program is accredited, produces qualified journeymen, feeds succession planning, and is continuously improved based on outcomes.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 4-year apprenticeship program that currently has 5+ active apprentices and has graduated at least 3 journeymen in the last 3 years scores a 4.

questions.question_text: How are training outcomes evaluated — is there a process to verify that training translates to improved on-the-job performance?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review post-training evaluation methods (Kirkpatrick levels). Look for evidence of Level 3 (behavior change) or Level 4 (results) evaluation.

scoring_rubric.1: No training evaluation occurs beyond attendance tracking.
scoring_rubric.2: Participant satisfaction surveys (Level 1) are the only evaluation method.
scoring_rubric.3: Post-training knowledge tests (Level 2) are conducted; pass rates are tracked.
scoring_rubric.4: On-the-job performance is evaluated post-training (Level 3); supervisors verify skill application within 90 days.
scoring_rubric.5: Training ROI is measured through performance metrics (Level 4); rework rates, MTTR, and safety incidents are correlated with training investments.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where supervisors complete a 90-day post-training observation checklist verifying technicians apply new skills on the job scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a dedicated training budget for the maintenance department, and is it protected from mid-year cuts?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review budget documents showing maintenance training line item. Compare planned vs. actual spend for the last 2 years.

scoring_rubric.1: No dedicated training budget exists; training is funded ad-hoc if at all.
scoring_rubric.2: Training budget exists but is routinely cut or redirected during the year.
scoring_rubric.3: Training budget exists and is generally maintained; some cuts occur during cost-reduction initiatives.
scoring_rubric.4: Training budget is established, protected, and fully spent annually; budget is based on training needs analysis.
scoring_rubric.5: Training budget is strategically set as a percentage of maintenance labor cost, protected at the executive level, and adjusted upward based on gap analysis.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a $150K annual training budget that spent 92% of budget last year and was not cut during a mid-year cost reduction scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are new maintenance employees (including contractors) required to complete a site-specific technical orientation covering critical equipment, safety systems, and local procedures before performing independent work?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the technical orientation checklist and completion records for the last 5 new hires or contractor onboardings.

scoring_rubric.1: No technical orientation exists; new personnel begin work immediately.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic safety orientation only; no site-specific technical content.
scoring_rubric.3: Technical orientation exists but is inconsistently applied; contractors may bypass it.
scoring_rubric.4: All new maintenance personnel (employees and contractors) complete a documented technical orientation before independent work; includes critical equipment familiarization.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive orientation includes equipment-specific training, competency verification, buddy system for first 30 days, and feedback mechanism to improve the program.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where every new contractor mechanic completes a 2-day technical orientation with equipment walkthroughs and must pass a written assessment before being released to work independently scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do supervisors and planners receive dedicated training in leadership, communication, planning/scheduling, and reliability principles?

scoring_rubric.1: No dedicated training for supervisors or planners; they learn on the job only.
scoring_rubric.2: Supervisors receive generic company leadership training; no maintenance-specific content.
scoring_rubric.3: Some maintenance-specific training for supervisors/planners (e.g., planning & scheduling fundamentals).
scoring_rubric.4: Structured development program for supervisors and planners covering leadership, R&M principles, planning/scheduling, and coaching skills.
scoring_rubric.5: Supervisors and planners follow a defined career development path with certifications (e.g., CMRP, CRL), mentoring, and ongoing professional development.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where all maintenance supervisors have completed a formal planning & scheduling course and a frontline leadership program within their first year in role scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a succession plan that identifies and develops future maintenance leaders and critical technical experts?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request succession planning documentation for maintenance leadership and critical technical roles. Verify development plans exist for identified successors.

scoring_rubric.1: No succession planning exists for maintenance roles.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal identification of potential successors; no documented development plans.
scoring_rubric.3: Key roles identified for succession planning; some development plans in place.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal succession plan covers all critical maintenance roles; identified successors have active, tracked development plans.
scoring_rubric.5: Succession planning is integrated with skills matrix, career pathing, and organizational strategy; pipeline produces ready-now candidates for all critical roles.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with documented successors for maintenance manager, reliability engineer, and lead planner roles — each with an active development plan reviewed quarterly — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the organization's ability to capture, retain, and transfer critical maintenance knowledge — especially from experienced technicians approaching retirement.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review knowledge capture processes, documented tribal knowledge, and retirement risk assessments for the maintenance workforce.

scoring_rubric.1: No knowledge capture process exists; critical knowledge is lost when experienced personnel leave.
scoring_rubric.2: Some informal knowledge sharing occurs; no documented process for knowledge retention.
scoring_rubric.3: A knowledge capture process exists (e.g., documented procedures, video recordings) but is applied inconsistently.
scoring_rubric.4: Systematic knowledge capture program covers critical assets and retiring experts; knowledge is documented, validated, and accessible.
scoring_rubric.5: Knowledge management is a strategic initiative; includes expert interviews, procedure development, decision guides, and is continuously updated and accessible via a searchable knowledge base.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that identifies technicians within 3 years of retirement, conducts structured knowledge-capture sessions covering their top 20 critical tasks, and publishes the results as enhanced SOPs scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are standard operating procedures (SOPs) and job plans current, accurate, and readily accessible to technicians at the point of work?
questions.evidence_guidance: Select 5 active PMs and verify that job plans are attached, current (reviewed within 2 years), and accessible to the technician (mobile device, printed copy, or kiosk).

scoring_rubric.1: SOPs/job plans do not exist or are severely outdated (>5 years without review).
scoring_rubric.2: SOPs exist for some tasks but many are outdated; technicians rarely reference them.
scoring_rubric.3: SOPs are available for most critical tasks; reviewed within the last 3 years; accessible in the shop or office.
scoring_rubric.4: SOPs are current, reviewed within 2 years, and accessible at point of work (mobile/tablet); technicians actively use them.
scoring_rubric.5: SOPs are living documents with embedded photos/videos, updated after every significant failure or RCA, and technicians contribute to their improvement.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor who finds 4 out of 5 sampled PMs have attached, current job plans that the technician can pull up on a tablet in the field scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is equipment-specific technical documentation (OEM manuals, P&IDs, wiring diagrams, nameplate data) organized, current, and accessible to maintenance personnel?
questions.evidence_guidance: Ask 3 technicians to locate the OEM manual and P&ID for a specific asset. Time the process and assess completeness.

scoring_rubric.1: Technical documentation is missing, disorganized, or inaccessible for most assets.
scoring_rubric.2: Some documentation exists but is scattered across filing cabinets, shared drives, and individual computers.
scoring_rubric.3: Documentation is organized in a central location (physical or digital) for critical assets; some gaps exist.
scoring_rubric.4: Comprehensive documentation library covers all major assets; linked to asset records in CMMS; accessible in the field.
scoring_rubric.5: Documentation is digitized, linked to CMMS asset hierarchy, includes redlined as-built drawings, and is maintained through a formal document control process.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who can locate the OEM manual, wiring diagram, and P&ID for a critical pump within 5 minutes using a structured digital library scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are lessons learned from equipment failures, RCAs, and project implementations documented and shared across the maintenance organization?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the lessons-learned database or repository. Check for entries from the last 12 months and evidence of dissemination (emails, toolbox talks, training sessions).

scoring_rubric.1: Lessons learned are not captured or shared.
scoring_rubric.2: Lessons learned are occasionally captured in RCA reports but not shared broadly.
scoring_rubric.3: A lessons-learned repository exists; sharing occurs through periodic safety/reliability meetings.
scoring_rubric.4: Lessons learned are systematically captured, categorized, and shared via toolbox talks, bulletins, and training; accessible to all maintenance personnel.
scoring_rubric.5: Lessons learned are integrated into SOPs, training curriculum, and design standards; cross-site sharing occurs in multi-site organizations; effectiveness is tracked.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that publishes a monthly reliability lessons-learned bulletin, discusses it in toolbox talks, and has updated 3 SOPs based on recent RCA findings scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do maintenance technicians have access to a searchable knowledge base or digital platform for troubleshooting guidance, historical repair data, and best practices?
questions.evidence_guidance: Verify the existence and accessibility of a knowledge base. Ask 2 technicians to demonstrate how they search for troubleshooting information.

scoring_rubric.1: No searchable knowledge base exists; technicians rely entirely on personal experience and colleagues.
scoring_rubric.2: Some information exists in shared drives or binders but is not searchable or well-organized.
scoring_rubric.3: A basic knowledge base exists (wiki, SharePoint) with troubleshooting guides for some equipment.
scoring_rubric.4: A searchable digital platform provides troubleshooting guides, historical work order data, and best practices; regularly updated and used by technicians.
scoring_rubric.5: AI-assisted knowledge platform provides contextual troubleshooting recommendations based on asset history, failure codes, and real-time condition data.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who can search a SharePoint-based knowledge base from a tablet, find a troubleshooting flowchart for a specific VFD fault code, and follow documented steps scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal document control process ensuring maintenance procedures, drawings, and technical references are reviewed and updated on a defined schedule?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the document control procedure. Check review dates on a sample of 10 critical maintenance procedures.

scoring_rubric.1: No document control process exists for maintenance documents.
scoring_rubric.2: Documents are created but not version-controlled or reviewed on schedule.
scoring_rubric.3: A document control process exists with defined review cycles but compliance is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Document control is enforced; critical procedures are reviewed on schedule with version tracking and approval workflows.
scoring_rubric.5: Fully digital document management system with automated review reminders, approval workflows, change tracking, and audit trails.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 80% of critical maintenance procedures show a review date within the last 2 years and have a documented approval chain scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are shift handover processes structured to ensure critical maintenance knowledge and ongoing job status are effectively transferred between shifts?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe a shift handover. Check for use of a structured handover log, discussion of open work, safety hazards, and equipment status.

scoring_rubric.1: No formal shift handover process; information is lost between shifts.
scoring_rubric.2: Verbal handover occurs but is unstructured; no written log.
scoring_rubric.3: A shift handover log exists; key information is recorded but quality varies.
scoring_rubric.4: Structured handover process with a standard checklist/log covering open work, safety, equipment status, and priorities; consistently applied.
scoring_rubric.5: Digital shift handover integrated with CMMS; includes equipment status board, critical work highlights, safety alerts, and is archived for reference.

questions.calibration_anchor: Observing a shift handover where outgoing and incoming supervisors walk through a structured log covering all open work, locked-out equipment, and priority changes scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What percentage of critical equipment has documented troubleshooting decision trees or diagnostic flowcharts available to maintenance technicians?
questions.evidence_guidance: Identify the top 20 critical assets. Determine how many have documented troubleshooting guides or decision trees attached to CMMS or accessible in the field.

scoring_rubric.1: No troubleshooting guides exist for any equipment.
scoring_rubric.2: Troubleshooting guides exist for <20% of critical equipment.
scoring_rubric.3: 20-50% of critical equipment has documented troubleshooting guides.
scoring_rubric.4: 50-80% of critical equipment has troubleshooting guides; guides are accessible in CMMS or at point of work.
scoring_rubric.5: >80% of critical equipment has detailed, validated troubleshooting guides integrated with CMMS and continuously improved based on failure history.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 60% of critical rotating equipment has flowchart-style troubleshooting guides linked to the CMMS PM task list scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance communities of practice or technical forums established to facilitate knowledge sharing across crews, shifts, or sites?

scoring_rubric.1: No forums or communities of practice exist; knowledge sharing is incidental.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional all-hands meetings discuss maintenance topics but no structured community of practice.
scoring_rubric.3: Periodic technical forums or lunch-and-learns occur; attendance is voluntary.
scoring_rubric.4: Structured communities of practice meet regularly (monthly/quarterly) with defined topics, cross-shift participation, and documented outcomes.
scoring_rubric.5: Multi-site communities of practice share best practices, conduct peer audits, and drive standardization across the organization.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that holds monthly reliability roundtable meetings where technicians present recent challenging repairs and share solutions, with documented action items, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: How well does the organization protect against single-point-of-knowledge risk — where only one person understands a critical equipment system or process?
questions.evidence_guidance: Identify 5 critical or complex equipment systems and determine how many people can independently maintain them. Review cross-training plans for single-point-of-knowledge risks.

scoring_rubric.1: Multiple critical systems depend on a single person's knowledge; no mitigation exists.
scoring_rubric.2: Single-point-of-knowledge risks are recognized informally but not systematically addressed.
scoring_rubric.3: Critical single-point-of-knowledge risks are identified; cross-training plans exist for some.
scoring_rubric.4: All critical systems have at least 2 trained individuals; cross-training plans are active and tracked.
scoring_rubric.5: No single-point-of-knowledge risks exist; all critical skills are covered by 3+ people, knowledge is documented, and coverage is monitored as a KPI.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that has identified all critical single-expert dependencies and has active cross-training plans with target completion dates for each scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the level of visible, active leadership commitment to maintenance and reliability excellence at the site management level.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review site leadership meeting agendas for maintenance/reliability topics. Interview 3 frontline supervisors about leadership engagement. Check for leadership participation in reliability reviews.

scoring_rubric.1: Site leadership views maintenance as a cost center to minimize; no engagement with reliability initiatives.
scoring_rubric.2: Leadership verbally supports reliability but does not allocate time, budget, or resources to it.
scoring_rubric.3: Leadership participates in periodic reliability reviews and has approved a reliability improvement budget.
scoring_rubric.4: Leadership actively champions reliability; participates in weekly reliability meetings, supports training investments, and holds managers accountable for reliability KPIs.
scoring_rubric.5: Leadership drives reliability as a strategic priority; sets long-term asset management goals, personally reviews bad actors, and integrates reliability into business decisions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A plant manager who attends weekly reliability meetings, reviews the top-10 bad actor list monthly, and has approved a 15% increase in PdM budget scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does site leadership actively support the weekly maintenance scheduling process by protecting the schedule from non-emergency break-ins?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review schedule compliance data for the last 13 weeks. Interview planners and operations supervisors about schedule break-in frequency and management response.

scoring_rubric.1: No weekly schedule exists or leadership does not support scheduling at all.
scoring_rubric.2: A weekly schedule is created but leadership allows frequent non-emergency break-ins; schedule compliance <50%.
scoring_rubric.3: Leadership supports scheduling in principle; schedule compliance 50-70% but break-ins are common.
scoring_rubric.4: Leadership actively protects the schedule; a formal break-in process exists requiring management approval; schedule compliance 70-85%.
scoring_rubric.5: Leadership enforces schedule discipline; break-in rate <5%; schedule compliance >85%; operations and maintenance jointly own the schedule.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where the operations manager must approve all non-emergency schedule changes, break-in rate is tracked at 12%, and schedule compliance averages 78% scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a documented and communicated maintenance and reliability strategy or policy endorsed by site or corporate leadership?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request the maintenance/reliability strategy document. Check for leadership signatures, communication evidence, and alignment with business objectives.

scoring_rubric.1: No documented maintenance or reliability strategy exists.
scoring_rubric.2: An informal or draft strategy exists but is not endorsed by leadership or communicated to the workforce.
scoring_rubric.3: A documented strategy exists, signed by leadership, but awareness among frontline staff is low.
scoring_rubric.4: Strategy is documented, endorsed, communicated to all levels, and translated into annual reliability improvement objectives.
scoring_rubric.5: Strategy is integrated with the overall business strategy, reviewed annually, drives budget allocation, and every employee can articulate how their role contributes to it.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a signed reliability strategy document that has been presented at all-hands meetings and translated into 5 specific annual improvement targets scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does leadership allocate sufficient budget for maintenance materials, spare parts, and tools without chronic shortages that delay work execution?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review MRO budget vs. actual for last 2 years. Check parts stockout rates and work orders delayed due to material unavailability.

scoring_rubric.1: Chronic budget shortfalls; frequent parts stockouts; technicians improvise or defer work due to lack of materials.
scoring_rubric.2: Budget exists but is insufficient; stockout rates >15%; critical spares frequently unavailable.
scoring_rubric.3: Budget generally adequate; stockout rate 5-15%; occasional delays but critical spares are usually available.
scoring_rubric.4: Budget is aligned with asset needs; stockout rate <5%; critical spares program is well-funded; material availability is tracked.
scoring_rubric.5: MRO budget is optimized using reliability data; zero critical-spare stockouts; budget includes provisions for reliability improvements and capital spare strategy.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 3% critical spare stockout rate, a funded insurance spares program, and no work orders delayed >1 week due to parts availability scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does leadership conduct regular maintenance and reliability performance reviews with accountability for improvement targets?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review agendas and minutes from the last 4 reliability performance review meetings. Check for action item tracking and accountability assignments.

scoring_rubric.1: No regular reliability performance reviews occur.
scoring_rubric.2: Maintenance metrics are reviewed informally or only when problems arise.
scoring_rubric.3: Monthly reliability reviews occur with KPI dashboards but accountability is limited.
scoring_rubric.4: Weekly/bi-weekly reliability reviews with leadership; action items tracked; managers accountable for improvement targets.
scoring_rubric.5: Tiered daily/weekly/monthly review cadence with cascading accountability; performance gaps trigger root-cause reviews; leadership models continuous improvement behavior.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site holding bi-weekly reliability reviews where the maintenance manager presents KPI trends, action items are tracked in a log, and the plant manager holds direct reports accountable scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do operations and maintenance leadership have a collaborative relationship with shared accountability for equipment reliability and availability?
questions.evidence_guidance: Interview operations and maintenance supervisors separately. Look for joint meetings, shared KPIs, and collaborative planning processes.

scoring_rubric.1: Adversarial relationship; maintenance blamed for all downtime; no joint planning.
scoring_rubric.2: Siloed relationship; minimal communication; separate KPIs and priorities.
scoring_rubric.3: Cooperative relationship; joint scheduling meetings occur but each department has separate priorities.
scoring_rubric.4: Collaborative relationship; shared reliability KPIs, joint planning and scheduling, regular operations-maintenance coordination meetings.
scoring_rubric.5: True partnership; operations and maintenance co-own reliability outcomes; operator care programs exist; joint RCAs and improvement teams are standard practice.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where operations and maintenance supervisors attend a daily joint planning meeting, share a downtime KPI, and co-chair a weekly scheduling meeting scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does leadership provide maintenance teams with adequate access to equipment for planned maintenance windows without chronic schedule erosion?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review PM compliance data. Interview planners about frequency of denied equipment access. Check outage/turnaround planning process.

scoring_rubric.1: Equipment access is rarely provided for planned maintenance; PMs are chronically deferred.
scoring_rubric.2: Equipment access is provided inconsistently; PM compliance <60% due to access issues.
scoring_rubric.3: Scheduled equipment access is generally provided; PM compliance 60-80%; some deferrals occur due to production pressure.
scoring_rubric.4: Equipment access is reliably provided per the weekly schedule; PM compliance >80%; deferrals require management approval.
scoring_rubric.5: Planned maintenance windows are sacrosanct; PM compliance >95%; operations proactively prepares equipment for maintenance.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where operations releases equipment for PM 85% of the time as scheduled, deferred PMs require operations manager approval, and PM compliance is 82% scores a 4.

questions.question_text: How effectively does leadership communicate the business case for reliability to all levels of the organization?

scoring_rubric.1: No communication about the business value of reliability occurs.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional mentions of reliability importance in general communications.
scoring_rubric.3: Leadership presents reliability business case at annual meetings; some frontline awareness.
scoring_rubric.4: Regular communication linking reliability performance to business outcomes; technicians understand how their work impacts production and safety.
scoring_rubric.5: All employees can articulate the cost of unreliability and how their role contributes to asset performance; reliability success stories are celebrated publicly.

questions.calibration_anchor: A frontline technician who can explain that last year's bearing failure cost $180K in lost production and that their precision alignment work prevents such failures scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does leadership invest in and support the adoption of modern maintenance technologies (mobility, sensors, analytics, drones, AR)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Inventory current technology tools available to maintenance. Review technology investment plans and recent technology implementations.

scoring_rubric.1: No investment in modern maintenance technology; all processes are paper-based.
scoring_rubric.2: CMMS exists but is not mobile; no investment in advanced monitoring or analytics.
scoring_rubric.3: Some technology investments (e.g., mobile CMMS, basic vibration program); adoption is limited.
scoring_rubric.4: Sustained technology investment with mobile CMMS, PdM technologies, and data analytics; adoption is widespread with user training.
scoring_rubric.5: Technology leadership with IoT sensors, AI-driven analytics, augmented reality for complex repairs, and a technology roadmap aligned with the asset management strategy.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with mobile CMMS deployed to all technicians, a mature vibration and thermography program, and a pilot project for wireless vibration sensors scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does leadership set and enforce clear expectations for work quality standards in maintenance execution?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review quality standards documentation. Check for quality inspection checklists, rework tracking, and leadership involvement in quality issues.

scoring_rubric.1: No defined work quality standards; 'get it running' mentality prevails.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal quality expectations exist but are not documented or consistently enforced.
scoring_rubric.3: Quality standards are documented for critical tasks; enforcement is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Clear quality standards with defined acceptance criteria; supervisors conduct quality inspections; rework is tracked and reviewed.
scoring_rubric.5: Quality-focused culture; every job has acceptance criteria, post-job quality reviews are standard, and leadership reviews quality trends as a KPI.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where supervisors sign off on quality checklists for critical repairs, rework rate is tracked monthly, and leadership reviews quality trends quarterly scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the integration of safety and reliability cultures — do personnel understand that reliable equipment is inherently safer equipment?
questions.evidence_guidance: Interview 5 maintenance personnel at different levels. Look for understanding of the link between equipment reliability and safety outcomes. Review safety incident data for equipment-related events.

scoring_rubric.1: Safety and reliability are treated as separate, unrelated programs.
scoring_rubric.2: Some awareness that equipment failures cause safety hazards; no formal linkage in programs.
scoring_rubric.3: Safety and reliability programs share some objectives; equipment-related safety incidents are tracked.
scoring_rubric.4: Integrated safety-reliability culture; safety risk assessments include equipment reliability; maintenance quality is recognized as a safety-critical activity.
scoring_rubric.5: Safety and reliability are inseparable in culture; all personnel articulate the connection; asset integrity is a core safety pillar with proactive metrics (PdM findings, defect identification).

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where maintenance personnel can explain how a missed PM on a pressure safety valve creates a safety risk, and this linkage is reinforced in safety meetings, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a proactive near-miss and hazard reporting culture in the maintenance department, and are reports acted upon?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review near-miss report volume and trends for the last 12 months. Check closure rates and time-to-action. Interview technicians about comfort level with reporting.

scoring_rubric.1: No near-miss reporting system or culture exists; reporting is seen as punitive.
scoring_rubric.2: Near-miss reporting system exists but volume is very low (<1 per month); technicians distrust the process.
scoring_rubric.3: Moderate near-miss reporting volume; reports are investigated but feedback to reporters is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Healthy near-miss reporting culture; >5 reports per month from maintenance; investigations complete within 30 days; reporters receive feedback.
scoring_rubric.5: Robust reporting culture; high volume with quality detail; rapid investigation and action; reporters are recognized; trend analysis drives preventive actions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A maintenance department generating 8+ near-miss reports per month, with 90% investigated within 2 weeks and feedback provided to reporters, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are lockout/tagout (LOTO) and energy isolation procedures rigorously followed for all maintenance activities, without shortcuts or workarounds?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe 3-5 active maintenance jobs requiring LOTO. Verify proper isolation, personal locks, try-start verification, and documentation.

scoring_rubric.1: LOTO procedures are not followed or do not exist; technicians work on energized equipment.
scoring_rubric.2: LOTO is applied inconsistently; shortcuts are common; supervision does not enforce compliance.
scoring_rubric.3: LOTO is generally followed for major work; some gaps on smaller tasks; procedures exist but may be outdated.
scoring_rubric.4: LOTO is rigorously applied for all work requiring isolation; procedures are current; periodic audits verify compliance; near-zero LOTO violations.
scoring_rubric.5: LOTO is a deeply embedded cultural norm; technicians self-enforce and coach peers; procedures include complex/group LOTO scenarios; annual competency verification.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor observing 5 jobs where every technician applies personal locks, verifies zero energy, and can explain the isolation procedure scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do maintenance personnel feel empowered to stop work if they identify an unsafe condition or a quality concern without fear of reprisal?
questions.evidence_guidance: Conduct anonymous interviews with 5+ technicians. Ask about specific instances of stop-work authority being exercised. Review any documented stop-work events.

scoring_rubric.1: Technicians feel pressured to continue work despite safety or quality concerns; stop-work authority is not recognized.
scoring_rubric.2: Stop-work authority exists on paper but technicians fear reprisal or schedule pressure discourages its use.
scoring_rubric.3: Stop-work authority is communicated; some technicians exercise it but others are hesitant.
scoring_rubric.4: Stop-work authority is actively encouraged; technicians feel safe exercising it; documented stop-work events are reviewed positively by leadership.
scoring_rubric.5: Stop-work is a cultural norm; leadership celebrates stop-work events; every new employee hears real examples; no production pressure overrides safety or quality.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who stopped a job due to a missing gasket specification, was thanked by the supervisor, and the event was shared in a safety meeting as a positive example scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are pre-job safety and planning briefs conducted before maintenance activities, covering hazards, LOTO requirements, procedures, and contingencies?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe 3 pre-job briefs. Check for hazard identification, LOTO review, procedure discussion, and crew engagement.

scoring_rubric.1: Pre-job briefs do not occur; technicians begin work without planning or hazard discussion.
scoring_rubric.2: Pre-job briefs are conducted for major jobs only; format is informal and content varies.
scoring_rubric.3: Pre-job briefs are standard for most jobs; a checklist is used but crew engagement varies.
scoring_rubric.4: All jobs include a structured pre-job brief with hazard ID, LOTO, procedure review, and team discussion; crew members actively participate.
scoring_rubric.5: Pre-job briefs are thorough, engaging, and include what-if scenarios; post-job debriefs also occur to capture lessons learned.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor observing a pre-job brief where the supervisor walks through the JSA, all crew members contribute hazard observations, and LOTO points are verified on P&ID scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a no-blame culture for reporting equipment failures, near-misses, and quality defects so that root causes are addressed rather than individuals punished?

scoring_rubric.1: Blame culture; technicians hide failures or defects to avoid consequences.
scoring_rubric.2: Some blame; technicians report selectively; investigations focus on 'who' not 'why'.
scoring_rubric.3: Management espouses no-blame but technicians are not fully convinced; reporting is cautious.
scoring_rubric.4: No-blame culture is practiced; RCA focuses on systems and processes; technicians openly share failure details without fear.
scoring_rubric.5: Just culture fully embedded; individuals are supported for honest reporting; only willful violations are addressed punitively; culture surveys confirm trust.

questions.calibration_anchor: A technician who honestly reports that a reassembled pump failed because they missed a tolerance and the organization uses this to improve the procedure rather than discipline the technician scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance-related safety incidents and equipment failures investigated jointly by safety, maintenance, and operations teams?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 3-5 recent safety incident or equipment failure investigations. Check team composition for cross-functional participation.

scoring_rubric.1: Safety investigates safety incidents; maintenance investigates equipment failures; no cross-functional approach.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional cross-functional investigation for major events; routine events investigated in silos.
scoring_rubric.3: Cross-functional investigation is standard for significant events; team composition varies.
scoring_rubric.4: All maintenance-safety incidents are investigated by cross-functional teams including safety, maintenance, operations, and engineering.
scoring_rubric.5: Integrated investigation process with defined roles, standardized methodology, shared recommendations, and joint accountability for action items.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where every recordable safety incident involving equipment has a cross-functional investigation team with safety, maintenance, and operations representatives scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do maintenance personnel maintain good housekeeping standards in work areas, shops, and around maintained equipment?
questions.evidence_guidance: Walk through maintenance shops, laydown areas, and 3-5 recently maintained equipment areas. Assess cleanliness, organization, and contamination control.

scoring_rubric.1: Poor housekeeping; shops and work areas are cluttered, dirty, and disorganized; tools and parts scattered.
scoring_rubric.2: Inconsistent housekeeping; some areas clean but many show neglect; no 5S program.
scoring_rubric.3: Reasonable housekeeping; shops are organized; work areas generally cleaned up after jobs; basic 5S implemented.
scoring_rubric.4: Good housekeeping standard; 5S program in place; shops are organized and clean; work areas are left clean after every job.
scoring_rubric.5: Exemplary housekeeping; 5S audited regularly; contamination control evident; maintenance areas are showplace-quality; housekeeping is a cultural value.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor finding the maintenance shop organized with shadow boards for tools, parts bins labeled, floors clean, and recently completed job sites cleared of debris scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is operator care (basic operator-performed maintenance such as cleaning, inspecting, lubricating) practiced as part of the reliability culture?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review operator care task lists and compliance records. Interview operators about their role in equipment care. Observe operator rounds.

scoring_rubric.1: Operators do not perform any maintenance tasks; 'I run it, you fix it' mentality.
scoring_rubric.2: Some operators informally clean or lubricate but no formal program exists.
scoring_rubric.3: Operator care program defined for some equipment; checklists exist but compliance is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal operator care program with defined tasks, checklists, training, and compliance tracking; operators take ownership of equipment cleanliness and basic care.
scoring_rubric.5: Mature TPM-style operator care with autonomous maintenance levels; operators detect and report early-stage defects; care activities continuously optimized based on failure data.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where operators perform daily CIL (Clean, Inspect, Lubricate) tasks on their equipment using standardized checklists with 85% compliance scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance and reliability achievements recognized and celebrated to reinforce desired behaviors and cultural norms?

scoring_rubric.1: No recognition of maintenance achievements; good work goes unnoticed.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional informal recognition; no structured program.
scoring_rubric.3: Some formal recognition events or awards exist but are infrequent and not well-publicized.
scoring_rubric.4: Regular recognition of reliability achievements (e.g., defect catches, PM innovation, RCA contributions); recognized in meetings and communications.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive recognition program tied to reliability behaviors; peer-nominated awards, public celebration of milestones, and direct link between recognition and cultural values.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that highlights a 'Reliability Champion of the Month' in all-hands meetings, recognizing a specific maintenance behavior or improvement, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the effectiveness of the maintenance organizational structure in supporting efficient work execution and clear accountability.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the maintenance organization chart. Assess span of control, reporting relationships, and whether planner, reliability, and supervisory roles are adequately staffed.

scoring_rubric.1: No defined maintenance organization; roles and responsibilities are unclear; excessive span of control.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic organizational structure exists but is under-resourced; supervisors manage >25 technicians; no dedicated planners or reliability roles.
scoring_rubric.3: Defined organization with supervisors, planners, and some reliability support; span of control is manageable (15-20).
scoring_rubric.4: Well-structured organization with appropriate supervisor-to-technician ratios (1:10-15), dedicated planners (1:20-30 technicians), and reliability engineering support.
scoring_rubric.5: Optimized organization with clear roles, appropriate ratios, dedicated reliability engineers, integrated planning function, and continuous organizational assessment.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with maintenance supervisors overseeing 12-15 technicians each, 2 dedicated planners for 40 technicians, and a reliability engineer on staff scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the maintenance planning function separated from day-to-day supervision so that planners can focus on future work preparation?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review organizational chart for planner positioning. Interview planners about how much time they spend on reactive coordination vs. future work planning.

scoring_rubric.1: No planning function exists; supervisors do all coordination and work preparation.
scoring_rubric.2: Planners exist but are routinely pulled into daily reactive work; <30% of their time is spent planning future work.
scoring_rubric.3: Planners are designated but still handle some daily coordination; 30-60% of time on future work planning.
scoring_rubric.4: Planners are organizationally separated from daily supervision; >70% of time is spent planning future work; clear role definition.
scoring_rubric.5: Planning function is fully dedicated and protected; planners spend >85% of time on future work; planning effectiveness is measured (planned vs. actual hours, kitting rates).

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where planners report to a planning supervisor rather than area supervisors, spend 75% of time on week+1 and beyond work preparation, and are not dispatched to daily issues scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a dedicated reliability engineering function with clear accountability for failure elimination and maintenance strategy optimization?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review organizational chart for reliability engineering roles. Check job descriptions, deliverables, and reporting structure.

scoring_rubric.1: No reliability engineering function exists.
scoring_rubric.2: Reliability activities are performed part-time by maintenance or process engineers with no formal accountability.
scoring_rubric.3: A dedicated reliability engineer role exists but is under-resourced or focused primarily on reactive failure analysis.
scoring_rubric.4: Reliability engineering function with defined roles, proactive responsibilities (RCM, FMEA, bad actor elimination), and clear deliverables.
scoring_rubric.5: Mature reliability engineering team with specializations (vibration, lubrication, RCA), proactive failure elimination programs, and integration with capital project design reviews.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a reliability engineer who leads the bad actor program, conducts FMEAs for critical assets, manages the PdM program, and reports to the maintenance/reliability manager scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are roles, responsibilities, and authorities for maintenance, operations, engineering, and storeroom clearly defined and understood across departments?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review RACI matrices or role descriptions for key maintenance processes. Interview personnel from different departments about their understanding of role boundaries.

scoring_rubric.1: Roles are undefined; frequent confusion about who does what; duplicated effort or dropped responsibilities.
scoring_rubric.2: Job descriptions exist but do not reflect actual practice; role boundaries are disputed.
scoring_rubric.3: Roles are generally understood within departments but cross-functional responsibilities are unclear.
scoring_rubric.4: Clear RACI or equivalent for key processes; cross-functional roles and handoffs are defined and understood.
scoring_rubric.5: Roles are documented, communicated, and periodically reviewed; process maps show clear handoffs; accountability is reinforced through performance management.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a documented RACI matrix for the work management process that clearly defines who requests, approves, plans, schedules, executes, and closes work scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the storeroom and MRO supply chain function organizationally positioned to support maintenance effectively with clear performance expectations?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review storeroom organizational reporting structure, staffing levels, and KPIs (fill rate, stockout rate, kitting completion). Interview planners about storeroom service quality.

scoring_rubric.1: No dedicated storeroom function; technicians self-serve from uncontrolled stockpiles.
scoring_rubric.2: Storeroom exists but is understaffed, unorganized, or reports through procurement with no maintenance alignment.
scoring_rubric.3: Storeroom is functional with basic controls; some KPIs tracked; reporting may be misaligned with maintenance priorities.
scoring_rubric.4: Storeroom is well-staffed and organized; KPIs include service level to maintenance; kitting and staging support the weekly schedule.
scoring_rubric.5: Storeroom is a strategic partner to maintenance; integrated with CMMS for automated reordering; fill rate >97%; kitting is standard; vendor-managed inventory for high-use items.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where the storeroom kits materials for 80% of planned jobs, tracks a 95% fill rate for critical spares, and reports service-level metrics to the maintenance manager scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What is the ratio of maintenance supervisors to technicians, and does it allow for effective coaching, quality oversight, and work coordination?
questions.evidence_guidance: Count the number of frontline maintenance supervisors and the number of technicians they directly supervise (including contractors).

scoring_rubric.1: Ratio >25:1; supervisors cannot provide effective oversight or coaching.
scoring_rubric.2: Ratio 20-25:1; supervisors are overwhelmed and primarily reactive.
scoring_rubric.3: Ratio 15-20:1; supervisors manage daily work but have limited time for coaching and proactive activities.
scoring_rubric.4: Ratio 10-15:1; supervisors balance daily coordination, coaching, quality checks, and proactive management.
scoring_rubric.5: Ratio 8-10:1; supervisors spend significant time coaching, conducting field observations, and driving improvement; administrative burden is minimized.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with 4 maintenance supervisors overseeing 50 technicians (12.5:1 ratio) where supervisors spend 40% of time in the field coaching and inspecting work scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the use of contract maintenance labor managed strategically with clear scope, quality expectations, and performance oversight?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review contractor management procedures, scoping documents, and performance evaluation records. Determine the ratio of contract to in-house maintenance labor.

scoring_rubric.1: Contract labor is used ad-hoc with no performance management, quality oversight, or strategic approach.
scoring_rubric.2: Contract labor is used frequently but scoping is vague, quality is not monitored, and cost is the primary selection criterion.
scoring_rubric.3: Contractor use is planned; basic contracts define scope and expectations; some quality oversight occurs.
scoring_rubric.4: Strategic contractor management with clear scope, quality requirements, safety expectations, performance scorecards, and regular reviews.
scoring_rubric.5: Optimized contractor strategy with long-term partnerships, integrated safety/quality programs, continuous improvement expectations, and total cost analysis (not just hourly rate).

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with contractor performance scorecards covering safety, quality, schedule adherence, and cost — reviewed quarterly with contractor management — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the maintenance organization have adequate administrative and coordination support so that supervisors and technicians are not burdened with excessive paperwork?

scoring_rubric.1: No administrative support; technicians and supervisors spend >30% of time on paperwork and data entry.
scoring_rubric.2: Minimal support; supervisors handle most administrative tasks personally.
scoring_rubric.3: Some administrative support available; supervisors still spend significant time on non-technical tasks.
scoring_rubric.4: Adequate administrative and coordination support; supervisors spend >70% of time on technical leadership and field activities.
scoring_rubric.5: Optimized support structure with administrative staff, mobile technology reducing paperwork, and process efficiency allowing supervisors to focus on coaching and quality.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where maintenance supervisors have clerical support for data entry and report generation, and can spend 75% of their time in the field, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal change management process for organizational changes that affect the maintenance department (reorganizations, headcount changes, role modifications)?

scoring_rubric.1: Organizational changes are made without process, communication, or impact assessment.
scoring_rubric.2: Changes are announced but not managed; no assessment of impact on maintenance capability.
scoring_rubric.3: Changes are communicated in advance; some impact assessment occurs but implementation support is limited.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal change management process for organizational changes; impact on maintenance capability assessed; transition plans developed.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive change management with stakeholder engagement, risk assessment, transition planning, post-change assessment, and continuous feedback.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that conducts a maintenance capability impact assessment before any headcount changes and develops a mitigation plan for identified risks scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the maintenance organization's headcount and skill mix periodically reviewed against workload requirements and asset criticality to ensure adequate coverage?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review any workforce planning analysis conducted in the last 2 years. Check for workload modeling, retirement projections, and gap analysis.

scoring_rubric.1: Headcount is determined by budget constraints only; no workload analysis.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal assessment of staffing adequacy; no quantitative workload analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: Periodic workforce review occurs but is not based on systematic workload modeling.
scoring_rubric.4: Annual workforce planning includes workload analysis, retirement projections, and skill mix assessment; gaps drive recruitment and training plans.
scoring_rubric.5: Continuous workforce optimization using CMMS workload data, predictive attrition modeling, and strategic workforce planning aligned with asset lifecycle plans.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that annually compares CMMS-derived labor demand against available technician hours by craft and uses the analysis to justify a hiring plan scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the overall maturity of the maintenance planning and scheduling process — from work identification through execution and closeout.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the work management process documentation. Observe a scheduling meeting. Check schedule compliance, plan accuracy, and backlog age metrics for the last 13 weeks.

scoring_rubric.1: No planning or scheduling process; all work is reactive and unplanned.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic planning exists for some work; no formal weekly scheduling; planned work <30%.
scoring_rubric.3: Formal planning and weekly scheduling process in place; planned work 30-60%; schedule compliance 50-70%.
scoring_rubric.4: Mature planning and scheduling with job plans, material coordination, weekly scheduling meetings, and schedule compliance 70-85%.
scoring_rubric.5: World-class planning and scheduling; >85% planned work, >85% schedule compliance, job plans with estimates, kitted materials, and continuous process improvement.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with dedicated planners producing job plans for 70% of work, weekly scheduling meetings with operations, and 78% schedule compliance scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance job plans developed with sufficient detail — including steps, safety requirements, materials, tools, labor estimates, and equipment specifications?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 10 job plans for detail and completeness. Check for safety steps, bill of materials, tool lists, labor hour estimates, and reference documents.

scoring_rubric.1: No job plans exist; work orders contain only a brief description.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic job plans exist for some recurring tasks; most lack materials, tools, or labor estimates.
scoring_rubric.3: Job plans include procedures and materials for most planned work; labor estimates are rough; some reference documents attached.
scoring_rubric.4: Comprehensive job plans with detailed steps, safety requirements, BOMs, tool lists, accurate labor estimates, and reference documents; feedback loop improves plans.
scoring_rubric.5: Job plans are living documents continuously improved with as-found/as-left feedback; include photos, vendor specifications, and task-level duration estimates based on historical data.

questions.calibration_anchor: A planner producing job plans with a 10-step procedure, linked BOM in CMMS, estimated 4-hour duration based on historical average, and attached OEM torque specifications scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What is the current weekly schedule compliance rate for planned maintenance work?
questions.evidence_guidance: Provide weekly schedule compliance data for the last 13 weeks. Calculate as (scheduled jobs completed ÷ scheduled jobs) × 100.

scoring_rubric.1: Schedule compliance is not measured or is below 30%.
scoring_rubric.2: Schedule compliance 30-50%.
scoring_rubric.3: Schedule compliance 50-70%.
scoring_rubric.4: Schedule compliance 70-85%.
scoring_rubric.5: Schedule compliance consistently >85% over the last 13 weeks.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site averaging 78% schedule compliance over the last quarter with a visible trend of improvement scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal weekly scheduling meeting attended by maintenance and operations that produces a frozen weekly schedule with defined break-in rules?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe or review minutes from the weekly scheduling meeting. Check for operations attendance, frozen schedule publication, and break-in approval process.

scoring_rubric.1: No scheduling meeting occurs.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal scheduling occurs but no regular meeting with operations; schedule is not frozen.
scoring_rubric.3: Weekly scheduling meeting occurs; operations attends sometimes; schedule is created but break-in rules are loose.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal weekly meeting with maintenance and operations; frozen schedule published by a defined day; break-ins require management approval.
scoring_rubric.5: Disciplined scheduling process with frozen schedule, break-in rate <5%, daily schedule update meetings, and full operations-maintenance collaboration.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a Thursday scheduling meeting producing a frozen Monday-Friday schedule, operations superintendent approval required for break-ins, and break-in rate tracked at 10% scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the maintenance backlog actively managed with aging analysis, prioritization, and regular review to prevent uncontrolled growth?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review backlog metrics: total backlog in weeks, aging distribution, and priority breakdown. Check for regular backlog review meetings.

scoring_rubric.1: Backlog is not measured or managed; open work orders accumulate without review.
scoring_rubric.2: Backlog size is known but not actively managed; aging work orders are not reviewed.
scoring_rubric.3: Backlog is tracked weekly; periodic reviews occur to cancel obsolete work; size is generally 4-8 weeks.
scoring_rubric.4: Active backlog management with weekly review, aging analysis, priority reassessment, and controlled size (2-4 weeks of executable work).
scoring_rubric.5: Optimized backlog at 2-4 weeks; aging >90 days is investigated; backlog feeds capacity planning and resource allocation; zero orders >180 days without justification.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 3-week backlog, monthly aging reviews that close stale work orders, and a Pareto chart showing backlog by priority and craft scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are work orders prioritized using a consistent, risk-based priority system that is understood and respected across the organization?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the work order priority matrix. Check distribution of priority codes for reasonableness. Interview supervisors and operations about priority assignment.

scoring_rubric.1: No priority system exists or everything is marked 'urgent'.
scoring_rubric.2: Priority codes exist but are not consistently applied; >50% of work orders are high priority.
scoring_rubric.3: Priority system defined and generally followed; some inflation of priorities; distribution is skewed but manageable.
scoring_rubric.4: Risk-based priority matrix in use; priorities assigned consistently; priority distribution is healthy (<10% emergency, 20-30% urgent, 60-70% routine).
scoring_rubric.5: Criticality-driven priority system integrated with asset criticality rankings; priority disputes are rare; data-driven priority assignment.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site using a 5-level priority matrix based on safety, environmental, and production impact where 8% of work orders are emergency and 65% are routine scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What percentage of total maintenance labor hours are spent on planned vs. unplanned (reactive/emergency) work?
questions.evidence_guidance: Extract CMMS data on work order type (planned/unplanned) and associated labor hours for the last 12 months.

scoring_rubric.1: Planned work <20% of total labor hours; organization is almost entirely reactive.
scoring_rubric.2: Planned work 20-40%.
scoring_rubric.3: Planned work 40-60%.
scoring_rubric.4: Planned work 60-80%.
scoring_rubric.5: Planned work >80% of total labor hours with emergency work <5%.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where CMMS data shows 70% of labor hours on planned work orders (PM, PdM, planned corrective) and 30% on reactive/emergency work scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are post-job reviews (planned vs. actual) conducted to improve future job plan accuracy and planning effectiveness?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review examples of job plans updated based on technician feedback. Check for a formal feedback loop process between execution and planning.

scoring_rubric.1: No post-job review or feedback to planners occurs.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional verbal feedback; job plans are rarely updated.
scoring_rubric.3: Technicians provide written feedback on some work orders; planners review periodically.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal feedback loop: technicians record actual hours, parts, and improvement suggestions; planners review and update job plans regularly.
scoring_rubric.5: Systematic post-job review process; job plan accuracy tracked as KPI; continuous improvement of plans based on execution data and technician input.

questions.calibration_anchor: A planner who reviews all completed work orders weekly, updates job plans when actual hours deviate >20% from estimates, and incorporates technician suggestions scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal work request screening and approval process that prevents duplicate, unnecessary, or incomplete work orders from entering the backlog?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the work request process. Check for screening criteria, duplicate checking, and approval workflow in CMMS.

scoring_rubric.1: Anyone can create work orders with no screening; backlog is full of duplicates and incomplete requests.
scoring_rubric.2: Work requests are reviewed but screening is inconsistent; duplicates are common.
scoring_rubric.3: Formal screening process with defined criteria; most requests are reviewed before approval; some duplicates still occur.
scoring_rubric.4: Robust screening with duplicate checking, completeness validation, and priority assignment before backlog entry; rejection/feedback loop for inadequate requests.
scoring_rubric.5: Automated screening in CMMS with duplicate detection, asset-linked request validation, and intelligent routing to planners; near-zero duplicates or incomplete requests.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where all work requests are screened by a planner within 48 hours, checked against open work orders for duplicates, and validated for completeness before approval scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance outages and turnarounds planned with sufficient lead time, scope discipline, and integrated scheduling?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the last major outage/turnaround plan. Check lead time, scope freeze date, critical path scheduling, and post-outage review.

scoring_rubric.1: Outages are unplanned or planned with minimal lead time; scope grows uncontrolled during execution.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic outage planning with 2-4 weeks lead time; scope is loosely defined; significant overruns are common.
scoring_rubric.3: Outage planning starts 6-8 weeks ahead; scope is defined but scope creep occurs; basic scheduling.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal outage planning process with 3-6 month lead time, scope freeze, critical path scheduling, daily progress tracking, and post-outage review.
scoring_rubric.5: World-class outage management with 6-12 month rolling plan, disciplined scope control, resource leveling, earned-value tracking, and continuous improvement from post-outage lessons.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that freezes turnaround scope 6 weeks before execution, uses critical path scheduling, holds daily progress meetings during the outage, and conducts a formal post-outage review scores a 4.
