questions.question_text: Rate the overall maturity and effectiveness of the preventive and predictive maintenance program in preventing unplanned equipment failures.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review PM/PdM program scope, compliance rates, and unplanned failure trends over the last 12-24 months. Calculate PM-to-CM ratio.

scoring_rubric.1: No formal PM/PdM program; all maintenance is reactive (run-to-failure).
scoring_rubric.2: Basic PM program exists but is calendar-based and rarely optimized; PdM is minimal or non-existent; PM compliance <60%.
scoring_rubric.3: PM program covers most critical assets with >70% compliance; basic PdM (vibration or thermography) in place; PM tasks are mostly OEM-recommended.
scoring_rubric.4: Optimized PM/PdM program with risk-based task selection; PM compliance >85%; PdM covers critical rotating and electrical assets; failure rates show downward trend.
scoring_rubric.5: World-class PM/PdM with RCM-based task strategies, condition-based intervals, >95% compliance, multiple PdM technologies, and demonstrated failure elimination results.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with PM compliance at 88%, vibration and thermography programs covering all critical rotating equipment, and a 15% reduction in unplanned failures over 2 years scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What is the current PM compliance rate (PMs completed on time vs. PMs scheduled)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Extract PM compliance data from CMMS for the last 13 weeks. Calculate as PMs completed within their due window ÷ total PMs due.

scoring_rubric.1: PM compliance <50% or not measured.
scoring_rubric.2: PM compliance 50-65%.
scoring_rubric.3: PM compliance 65-80%.
scoring_rubric.4: PM compliance 80-90%.
scoring_rubric.5: PM compliance consistently >90% with overdue PMs investigated for root cause.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site reporting 85% PM compliance over the last quarter with a Pareto chart of overdue PMs by reason code scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are PM task lists periodically reviewed and optimized based on failure history, condition monitoring data, and RCM/FMEA analysis to eliminate non-value-added tasks?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review evidence of PM optimization activities (PMO, RCM reviews). Check for eliminated, consolidated, or frequency-changed PMs in the last 2 years.

scoring_rubric.1: PM tasks have never been reviewed since initial setup; many are OEM-recommended without validation.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional PM reviews occur but are not systematic; tasks added but rarely eliminated.
scoring_rubric.3: Periodic PM reviews (every 2-3 years) based on technician feedback and failure data; some tasks optimized.
scoring_rubric.4: Systematic PM optimization (PMO/RCM) program with regular reviews; tasks are added, modified, or eliminated based on failure analysis and condition data.
scoring_rubric.5: Continuous PM optimization using reliability-centered analysis, condition data trends, and cost-benefit analysis; PM program demonstrates measurable cost and reliability improvement.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that has completed PMO reviews on the top 20 critical assets in the last 2 years, eliminated 15% of non-value-added tasks, and shifted 10% to condition-based scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Which predictive maintenance technologies are deployed and what percentage of critical assets are covered by PdM routes?
questions.evidence_guidance: List PdM technologies in use (vibration, thermography, ultrasonics, oil analysis, motor testing). Determine coverage percentage for critical asset classes.

scoring_rubric.1: No PdM technologies deployed.
scoring_rubric.2: One PdM technology (typically vibration or thermography) covers <30% of applicable critical assets.
scoring_rubric.3: 2-3 PdM technologies deployed; 30-60% of applicable critical assets covered.
scoring_rubric.4: 3-4 PdM technologies deployed; >60% of applicable critical assets covered; results drive work orders.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive PdM program with 4+ technologies, >80% critical asset coverage, certified analysts, integrated data analysis, and demonstrated failure avoidance.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site using vibration analysis, thermography, and oil analysis covering 70% of critical rotating equipment, with monthly reports driving corrective work orders, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are PM findings and condition data used to generate follow-up corrective work orders that are tracked through planning and execution?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review PM work orders for documented findings. Trace findings to follow-up corrective work orders. Check the percentage of PM-generated CMs that are planned and completed.

scoring_rubric.1: PM findings are not documented or acted upon.
scoring_rubric.2: Some findings are noted on PM work orders but follow-up is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.3: Findings are documented; follow-up work orders are created for significant issues but tracking is informal.
scoring_rubric.4: PM findings systematically generate follow-up work orders linked to the PM; tracked through planning, scheduling, and execution.
scoring_rubric.5: PM findings are categorized by severity, generate prioritized follow-up work, and are analyzed for trends to improve PM effectiveness; closure rates are tracked.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 90% of significant PM findings result in linked corrective work orders that are planned and scheduled within the next maintenance cycle scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the lubrication program managed as an engineered reliability activity with specified lubricants, quantities, frequencies, and contamination control?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the lubrication program: lubricant specifications by equipment, lubrication routes, contamination control practices, and oil analysis program.

scoring_rubric.1: No organized lubrication program; lubrication is ad-hoc or performed only during other PMs.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic lubrication routes exist but lubricant specifications are not verified; greasing is time-based with no quantity control.
scoring_rubric.3: Lubrication program with specified lubricants and frequencies; some contamination control; oil analysis on critical equipment.
scoring_rubric.4: Engineered lubrication program with OEM-verified lubricants, calculated re-greasing quantities, color-coded containers, desiccant breathers on critical reservoirs, and oil analysis with action limits.
scoring_rubric.5: Best-in-class lubrication with centralized lube room, cleanliness targets (ISO codes), real-time oil condition monitoring, ultrasonic-assisted greasing, and certified lubrication technicians.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a lubrication survey completed for all rotating equipment, color-coded lubricant dispensing, desiccant breathers on gearboxes, and quarterly oil analysis with action limits scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are asset criticality rankings established and used to determine the appropriate maintenance strategy (run-to-failure, PM, PdM, or redesign) for each asset?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the asset criticality ranking methodology and results. Verify that maintenance strategies differ based on criticality (e.g., critical assets have PdM; low-criticality assets run to failure).

scoring_rubric.1: No asset criticality ranking exists; all assets are treated equally.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal criticality understanding exists but is not documented or used for strategy selection.
scoring_rubric.3: Criticality rankings exist for some assets; PM strategies are somewhat differentiated by criticality.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal criticality ranking (based on safety, environmental, production impact) covers all significant assets; strategies are tailored by criticality level.
scoring_rubric.5: Dynamic criticality assessment integrated with CMMS; strategies optimized by criticality and risk; regular reviews update criticality based on changing conditions and failure data.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a documented criticality matrix covering all assets, where Criticality A assets have PdM and detailed PMs while Criticality C assets are run-to-failure, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do PdM analysts produce actionable reports with clear severity ratings, recommended actions, and timeframes for corrective work?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 5 recent PdM reports. Check for severity classification, recommended actions, and urgency timeframes. Verify that recommendations convert to planned work orders.

scoring_rubric.1: PdM reports are not generated or are purely technical without actionable recommendations.
scoring_rubric.2: Reports are generated but lack severity ratings or clear action recommendations.
scoring_rubric.3: Reports include severity and recommendations; some delay in converting to work orders.
scoring_rubric.4: PdM reports have standardized severity ratings (Alert/Alarm/Critical), specific corrective actions, recommended timeframes, and are consistently converted to planned work orders.
scoring_rubric.5: PdM reports are integrated into the CMMS workflow; automatic work order generation for confirmed defects; trending dashboards show P-F curve management.

questions.calibration_anchor: A PdM analyst producing a monthly report with Green/Yellow/Red severity coding, specific repair recommendations, and timeframes that are directly converted to work orders by the planner scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the PM program integrated with the CMMS such that PM schedules are auto-generated, compliance is tracked, and overdue PMs are escalated?
questions.evidence_guidance: Verify PM generation in CMMS. Check for auto-scheduling, overdue alerts, and compliance dashboards.

scoring_rubric.1: PMs are tracked manually (spreadsheet or paper); no CMMS automation.
scoring_rubric.2: CMMS generates PM work orders but scheduling and compliance tracking are manual.
scoring_rubric.3: CMMS auto-generates PMs on schedule; basic compliance reporting available.
scoring_rubric.4: PM lifecycle managed in CMMS: auto-generation, scheduling integration, compliance tracking, overdue alerts to supervisors, and dashboard reporting.
scoring_rubric.5: Fully automated PM management with dynamic scheduling based on meter readings, compliance alerts with escalation workflows, and predictive workload balancing.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where CMMS auto-generates PM work orders, produces a weekly compliance report, and sends automatic alerts for PMs overdue >7 days scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Has the organization conducted formal RCM or FMEA studies on its most critical equipment to determine optimal failure management strategies?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review RCM or FMEA study documentation. Verify implementation of recommendations and ongoing review of results.

scoring_rubric.1: No RCM or FMEA studies have been conducted.
scoring_rubric.2: One or two pilot studies completed but recommendations are not fully implemented.
scoring_rubric.3: RCM/FMEA completed on a few critical asset classes; recommendations partially implemented.
scoring_rubric.4: RCM/FMEA completed on top 10-20 critical assets; recommendations implemented and tracked; results show measurable improvement.
scoring_rubric.5: RCM/FMEA is the standard methodology for all critical assets; living analyses updated based on failure data; culture of reliability-centered thinking is pervasive.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that has completed FMEA studies on 15 critical asset types, implemented 80% of recommended task changes, and can show a 20% reduction in failures on analyzed assets scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the overall quality of maintenance work execution — do jobs get done right the first time, on time, and to standard?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review rework rate, callback rate, and MTTR trends. Observe 2-3 active maintenance jobs for adherence to procedures, quality standards, and safety practices.

scoring_rubric.1: Work quality is poor; high rework rates (>15%); jobs frequently not completed to standard; workarounds are common.
scoring_rubric.2: Inconsistent quality; some technicians produce good work but rework rate is 10-15%; procedures are not consistently followed.
scoring_rubric.3: Acceptable quality for most work; rework rate 5-10%; procedures followed for critical tasks; some quality checks occur.
scoring_rubric.4: Good quality standard; rework rate <5%; procedures followed consistently; supervisors conduct quality inspections on critical work.
scoring_rubric.5: Excellent quality; rework rate <2%; every job has defined acceptance criteria; post-job quality verification is standard; quality culture is evident.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 4% rework rate, supervisor sign-off on critical repairs, and technicians consistently following procedures as observed during field audits scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are proper tools, equipment, and consumables available and staged for maintenance work before the job begins?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe 3-5 job startups. Check whether materials are kitted, tools are available, and technicians can begin work without delays for material procurement.

scoring_rubric.1: Tools and materials are never staged; technicians spend significant time searching for parts and tools.
scoring_rubric.2: Some tools available in personal toolboxes; materials rarely staged; frequent job delays for material procurement.
scoring_rubric.3: Tools are generally available; materials are partially kitted for planned jobs; some delays still occur.
scoring_rubric.4: Materials kitted and staged for >70% of planned work; specialty tools identified in job plans; minimal wrench-time lost to material procurement.
scoring_rubric.5: Full kitting and staging for all planned work; tool cribs with check-out systems; wrench-time studies show >55% productive time; material delivery to point of work.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor observing that 4 out of 5 planned jobs had materials kitted and staged in the storeroom before the job was dispatched scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal work order closeout process that captures failure codes, actual labor hours, parts used, and as-found/as-left conditions?
questions.evidence_guidance: Audit 20 recently closed work orders for data completeness. Check for failure codes, actual hours, parts charged, and condition narrative.

scoring_rubric.1: Work orders are closed with minimal or no data; no closeout process exists.
scoring_rubric.2: Some data captured at closeout but quality is poor; failure codes rarely used; hours are estimated.
scoring_rubric.3: Closeout process defined; 50-70% of work orders have adequate data; failure codes used inconsistently.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal closeout process enforced; >80% of work orders have complete data including failure codes, actual hours, parts, and condition notes.
scoring_rubric.5: >95% data completeness at closeout; planners review and validate before final close; data quality audits occur monthly; closeout data drives reliability analysis.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where planners review work orders before closure, 85% have proper failure codes and actual hours, and monthly data quality reports are published scores a 4.

questions.question_text: What is the estimated wrench time (direct productive work time as a percentage of available time) for maintenance technicians?
questions.evidence_guidance: Conduct or review a wrench-time study (work sampling) or use supervisor estimates. Identify top time-wasting activities.

scoring_rubric.1: Wrench time <25% or never measured; excessive waiting, traveling, and searching for materials.
scoring_rubric.2: Wrench time 25-35%; significant time lost to waiting for parts, permits, and equipment access.
scoring_rubric.3: Wrench time 35-45%; common delays identified but not systematically addressed.
scoring_rubric.4: Wrench time 45-55%; systematic effort to reduce delays; material kitting, job staging, and permit pre-processing in place.
scoring_rubric.5: Wrench time >55%; world-class work execution efficiency; all barriers to productive time systematically eliminated.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that has conducted a work sampling study showing 48% wrench time and has implemented kitting and permit pre-processing to address identified delays scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do maintenance technicians consistently follow approved procedures and job plans during work execution rather than relying on memory or personal methods?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe 3-5 active maintenance jobs. Ask technicians to show the job plan/procedure they are following. Check for signed-off procedure steps.

scoring_rubric.1: Procedures are not used; technicians work from memory or personal preference.
scoring_rubric.2: Procedures exist but are in the shop/office; technicians rarely carry them to the job.
scoring_rubric.3: Procedures are available and sometimes referenced; critical steps may be followed but documentation is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Technicians carry procedures to the job, follow documented steps, and sign off on critical hold points.
scoring_rubric.5: Procedure use is a cultural norm; technicians self-verify against procedures, report discrepancies, and contribute to procedure improvements.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor observing a technician with the job plan displayed on a tablet, following steps sequentially, and signing off after a critical torque step scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance rework and repeat failure events tracked, analyzed, and acted upon to eliminate recurring quality issues?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review rework tracking data and RCA reports for maintenance-induced failures. Check for corrective actions targeting installation quality, procedure gaps, or training needs.

scoring_rubric.1: Rework is not tracked or recognized as a metric.
scoring_rubric.2: Rework is informally acknowledged but not systematically tracked.
scoring_rubric.3: Rework rate is tracked as a metric; some investigation of repeat failures occurs.
scoring_rubric.4: Rework events trigger investigation; root causes are categorized (procedure, training, parts quality, etc.); corrective actions are tracked to closure.
scoring_rubric.5: Rework is a primary quality KPI; every rework event is investigated; systemic causes are addressed; rework rate shows continuous decline.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that tracks rework rate monthly, investigates all rework events, categorizes root causes, and can show rework declining from 8% to 4% over 18 months scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are critical quality hold points defined in maintenance procedures (e.g., torque verification, alignment checks, leak tests) with mandatory supervisor or quality sign-off?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 5 job plans for critical maintenance tasks. Check for defined hold points, acceptance criteria, and sign-off requirements.

scoring_rubric.1: No hold points or acceptance criteria defined in maintenance procedures.
scoring_rubric.2: Some critical tasks have informal quality checkpoints but no mandatory sign-off.
scoring_rubric.3: Hold points defined for some critical tasks; sign-off is sometimes obtained.
scoring_rubric.4: Critical quality hold points with acceptance criteria defined in job plans for all critical work; supervisor sign-off required before proceeding.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive hold-point system with independent quality verification; as-found/as-left data recorded at each hold point; QA/QC function reviews critical maintenance work.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where pump rebuild procedures include mandatory hold points for bearing clearance, alignment tolerance, and seal face inspection — each requiring supervisor sign-off — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is equipment properly commissioned and tested after maintenance before being returned to operations?
questions.evidence_guidance: Observe the return-to-service process for a maintained asset. Check for functional testing, leak checks, vibration baseline, and formal handover to operations.

scoring_rubric.1: Equipment is returned to operations without testing or commissioning.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic bump-test or visual check only; no documented commissioning process.
scoring_rubric.3: Standard commissioning steps performed for critical equipment; documentation is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal commissioning procedure for all significant work: functional test, leak check, vibration/alignment verification, and documented handover to operations.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive return-to-service protocol with checklists, baseline data collection, conditional monitoring verification, and operations acceptance sign-off.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor observing a pump returned to service with a documented leak check, post-maintenance vibration reading compared to baseline, and operations sign-off scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are incoming spare parts and materials inspected for quality and specification compliance before being accepted into the storeroom or installed?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review incoming inspection procedures and rejection records. Check for specification verification on critical spares.

scoring_rubric.1: No incoming inspection process; parts are accepted and installed without verification.
scoring_rubric.2: Visual inspection only for obvious damage; no specification verification.
scoring_rubric.3: Basic inspection against purchase order specifications for high-value items.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal incoming inspection for all critical spares against documented specifications; rejection records maintained; vendor quality issues tracked.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive incoming inspection program with measurement verification, material certifications, vendor quality ratings, and integration with reliability data on spare part failures.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that inspects all incoming bearings, seals, and motor components against documented specifications, maintains rejection records, and notifies procurement of vendor quality issues scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a management of change (MOC) process for maintenance activities that alter equipment configuration, materials, or operating parameters?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the MOC procedure. Check for recent examples of maintenance-initiated MOCs. Verify that technicians and supervisors understand when MOC is required.

scoring_rubric.1: No MOC process for maintenance; equipment modifications occur without review or documentation.
scoring_rubric.2: MOC process exists but maintenance personnel are not trained on it; rarely applied to maintenance work.
scoring_rubric.3: MOC applied to obvious equipment modifications; gray areas (like-for-like substitutions, material changes) are not consistently managed.
scoring_rubric.4: Clear MOC criteria for maintenance; technicians know when to trigger MOC; reviews include safety, reliability, and engineering input.
scoring_rubric.5: Robust MOC process integrated with CMMS and work order workflow; all non-like-for-like changes reviewed; pre-startup safety review after significant changes.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where maintenance supervisors can articulate when MOC is required, 10+ maintenance MOCs were processed last year, and a pre-startup review followed each significant change scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the overall effectiveness of the CMMS/EAM system in supporting maintenance planning, execution, and analysis.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review CMMS utilization metrics (users, WOs created, features used). Interview planners, supervisors, and technicians about CMMS effectiveness and pain points.

scoring_rubric.1: No CMMS in place or system exists but is not used for work management.
scoring_rubric.2: CMMS used primarily for work order tracking; many features underutilized; data quality is poor.
scoring_rubric.3: CMMS used for work orders, PM scheduling, and basic reporting; asset hierarchy exists but has gaps; moderate user adoption.
scoring_rubric.4: CMMS is well-utilized for full work management lifecycle; good data quality; mobile access for technicians; reporting supports decision-making.
scoring_rubric.5: CMMS is a strategic asset management tool; full feature utilization; integrated with PdM, procurement, and financials; advanced analytics and dashboards; continuous improvement of system use.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where all work flows through CMMS, planners use job plan libraries, technicians close work orders via mobile, and management reviews weekly KPI dashboards from CMMS data scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the asset hierarchy in the CMMS complete, accurate, and structured to support meaningful reliability analysis (functional location → equipment → component)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the CMMS asset hierarchy for a sample process area. Verify depth (functional location, equipment, component), accuracy, and linkage to PM schedules and work orders.

scoring_rubric.1: No asset hierarchy or a flat equipment list with no structure.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic hierarchy exists but has significant gaps; many assets not registered; no component-level detail.
scoring_rubric.3: Hierarchy covers most production assets with functional location and equipment levels; some component detail for critical assets.
scoring_rubric.4: Complete hierarchy for all maintainable assets with functional location, equipment, and component levels for critical assets; accurate nameplate data; linked to PM schedules.
scoring_rubric.5: Dynamic asset hierarchy with component-level tracking for all critical assets; BOMs attached; linked to drawings, manuals, and spare parts; regularly audited for accuracy.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 3-level hierarchy covering all process areas, equipment-level records for all maintainable assets, and component-level records for critical pumps and compressors scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Do all maintenance work orders flow through the CMMS — from request through planning, scheduling, execution, and closeout — with no significant offline or paper-based workarounds?
questions.evidence_guidance: Verify that all work types (emergency, corrective, PM, PdM, project) are captured in CMMS. Interview supervisors about any offline work tracking.

scoring_rubric.1: CMMS is not used or most work is tracked outside the system (spreadsheets, paper, whiteboards).
scoring_rubric.2: CMMS captures some work but significant work occurs without work orders; emergency work rarely documented.
scoring_rubric.3: Most planned work flows through CMMS; some emergency and small jobs bypass the system.
scoring_rubric.4: All significant work flows through CMMS including emergencies; work order capture rate >90%; minor tasks (e.g., operator adjustments) may be exceptions.
scoring_rubric.5: >98% of all maintenance work captured in CMMS; no parallel tracking systems; emergency work orders created within 24 hours of execution.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where supervisors confirm that all maintenance work including emergencies gets a work order in CMMS, with emergency WOs created retroactively within the same shift, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the CMMS accessible to maintenance technicians in the field via mobile devices or kiosks to reduce paperwork and improve data timeliness?
questions.evidence_guidance: Verify mobile CMMS deployment. Check the number of mobile licenses, device availability, and usage rates. Ask technicians about their experience.

scoring_rubric.1: No mobile or field access to CMMS; all interaction is via desktop in the office.
scoring_rubric.2: CMMS is web-accessible but technicians don't have field devices; data entry occurs after returning to the office.
scoring_rubric.3: Some mobile access via shared tablets or kiosks; adoption is partial.
scoring_rubric.4: Mobile CMMS deployed to all technicians on personal or assigned devices; work orders received and closed in the field; offline capability available.
scoring_rubric.5: Full mobile CMMS with barcode/QR scanning, photo attachment, voice-to-text notes, real-time data sync, and high user adoption rates (>80% field usage).

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where every technician has a ruggedized tablet with CMMS mobile app, can view and close work orders in the field, and 70% of WOs are closed from the mobile device scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are CMMS failure code hierarchies (problem, cause, remedy) configured to support meaningful failure analysis and reliability trending?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the failure code structure in CMMS. Check for problem-cause-remedy hierarchy, ISO 14224 alignment, and usage rates on closed work orders.

scoring_rubric.1: No failure codes configured in CMMS.
scoring_rubric.2: Failure codes exist but are a flat list with unclear definitions; usage rate <30% of closed WOs.
scoring_rubric.3: Hierarchical failure codes configured; usage rate 30-60%; some inconsistency in application.
scoring_rubric.4: Well-structured failure codes (problem → cause → remedy) aligned with ISO 14224; usage rate >70%; codes enable meaningful Pareto analysis.
scoring_rubric.5: ISO 14224 compliant failure coding with equipment-class-specific codes; >90% usage rate; automated validation; failure code data drives reliability improvement decisions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 3-level failure code hierarchy (what failed → why it failed → what was done), used on 75% of corrective work orders, enabling quarterly failure Pareto analysis scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is CMMS training provided to all users with role-based content and periodic refresher sessions?

scoring_rubric.1: No CMMS training provided; users learn informally or not at all.
scoring_rubric.2: Initial training provided for some users; no refresher training; new hires learn by observing.
scoring_rubric.3: Initial training for all users; some role-based content; refresher training is ad-hoc.
scoring_rubric.4: Role-based CMMS training for all users (technicians, planners, supervisors, managers); annual refresher; training materials current with system version.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive CMMS training program with role-based curricula, e-learning modules, hands-on practice environments, competency verification, and training for new features.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that provides 4-hour role-specific CMMS training to all new maintenance personnel, with annual refresher sessions covering system updates and best practices, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the CMMS integrated with other enterprise systems (financials, procurement, HR, process control) to avoid duplicate data entry and enable holistic asset management?
questions.evidence_guidance: Identify integrations between CMMS and other systems. Verify that data flows automatically and is not re-keyed manually.

scoring_rubric.1: CMMS is a standalone system with no integrations.
scoring_rubric.2: Manual data exchange (export/import) between CMMS and financials or procurement.
scoring_rubric.3: 1-2 system integrations active (e.g., procurement or financials); some manual data transfer remains.
scoring_rubric.4: CMMS integrated with financials, procurement, and HR; data flows automatically; asset costs and personnel data are linked.
scoring_rubric.5: Full enterprise integration including process historian, condition monitoring systems, GIS, and IoT platforms; real-time data exchange; single source of truth for asset data.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where CMMS purchase requisitions flow automatically to the ERP procurement module, labor hours sync with payroll, and asset costs are visible in the financial system scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a designated CMMS system administrator or power user responsible for configuration, data quality, and user support?
questions.evidence_guidance: Identify who manages the CMMS day-to-day. Check for system administration procedures, help desk process, and configuration change management.

scoring_rubric.1: No designated system administrator; CMMS configuration changes are unmanaged.
scoring_rubric.2: IT manages CMMS infrastructure but no maintenance-side administrator exists for configuration and data quality.
scoring_rubric.3: A part-time CMMS administrator or power user exists within maintenance; basic support provided.
scoring_rubric.4: Dedicated CMMS administrator manages configuration, data quality, user support, and training; configuration changes follow a change management process.
scoring_rubric.5: CMMS governance team with administrator, data steward, and user champions; continuous improvement of system utilization; regular audits and optimization.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a dedicated CMMS administrator who manages user accounts, validates data quality monthly, responds to user issues within 24 hours, and follows a change management process scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the CMMS produce standard reports and dashboards that maintenance and operations leadership use for decision-making?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review standard CMMS reports and dashboards. Verify that leadership uses them in weekly/monthly review meetings.

scoring_rubric.1: No standard reports produced from CMMS; data is extracted manually for occasional analysis.
scoring_rubric.2: A few basic reports exist but are not regularly used for management decisions.
scoring_rubric.3: Standard weekly/monthly reports available (backlog, PM compliance, WO count); used in some meetings.
scoring_rubric.4: Comprehensive dashboard suite covering key KPIs; reports used in weekly reliability reviews and monthly management reviews; drill-down capability available.
scoring_rubric.5: Real-time dashboards with KPI trending, automated distribution, exception-based alerts, and advanced analytics; reports directly drive management actions and resource allocation.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where the maintenance manager opens a CMMS dashboard every Monday showing PM compliance, backlog status, schedule compliance, and emergency work ratio — and uses it to guide the weekly meeting — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are spare parts bills of materials (BOMs) attached to equipment records in the CMMS with accurate part numbers and storeroom linkages?
questions.evidence_guidance: Check 10 critical asset records in CMMS for attached BOMs. Verify that part numbers link to storeroom inventory items.

scoring_rubric.1: No BOMs attached to equipment records in CMMS.
scoring_rubric.2: BOMs exist for <20% of critical equipment; many are incomplete or outdated.
scoring_rubric.3: BOMs exist for 20-50% of critical equipment; linked to storeroom for some items.
scoring_rubric.4: BOMs exist for >50% of critical equipment with accurate part numbers linked to storeroom inventory; planners use BOMs for job planning.
scoring_rubric.5: >80% of critical equipment has complete, validated BOMs linked to inventory; automated reorder triggers for critical spares; BOM maintenance is a defined process.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 60% of critical pumps have BOMs in CMMS with part numbers linked to storeroom stock, enabling planners to generate material lists from the BOM, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the overall quality and reliability of maintenance data in the CMMS — is it trusted and used for decision-making?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review data quality metrics (completeness, accuracy, timeliness). Interview managers about their confidence in CMMS data. Check for data quality audit processes.

scoring_rubric.1: CMMS data is generally unreliable; managers do not trust it for decisions; parallel tracking systems exist.
scoring_rubric.2: Data quality varies widely; some metrics are useful but overall confidence is low; significant manual validation required.
scoring_rubric.3: Data quality is acceptable for basic reporting; some managers use CMMS data for decisions but others maintain separate tracking.
scoring_rubric.4: CMMS data is trusted and used as the primary source for reliability decisions; regular data quality checks occur; accuracy >85%.
scoring_rubric.5: CMMS is the single source of truth; data quality program with defined standards, automated validation, regular audits, and >95% accuracy across key fields.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where maintenance managers use CMMS data as their primary decision-making source, monthly data quality reports show >85% field completeness, and no parallel spreadsheets exist scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are work order data fields (equipment, failure codes, labor hours, parts used) validated for completeness and accuracy before work orders are closed?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the work order closeout process. Check for mandatory field validation, planner review, and data quality audit results.

scoring_rubric.1: No validation occurs; work orders are closed with empty or incorrect fields.
scoring_rubric.2: Minimal system validation (e.g., status change) but no content validation.
scoring_rubric.3: Some mandatory fields enforced by CMMS; planner spot-checks occasionally.
scoring_rubric.4: CMMS enforces mandatory fields for closure; planners review work orders before final close; monthly data quality audits identify gaps.
scoring_rubric.5: Multi-level validation: CMMS enforces mandatory fields, planners validate data quality, and automated rules flag anomalies; data quality KPI tracked weekly.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where CMMS requires equipment, failure code, and actual hours before WO closure, and planners review 100% of corrective WOs for data quality before final close, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the equipment master data in CMMS (nameplate data, specifications, serial numbers, installation dates) accurate and complete for critical assets?
questions.evidence_guidance: Select 10 critical equipment records and field-verify nameplate data accuracy. Check for completeness of key fields (manufacturer, model, serial, install date, specifications).

scoring_rubric.1: Equipment master data is missing or severely outdated; <30% of records have basic data.
scoring_rubric.2: 30-50% of critical equipment records have basic nameplate data; many fields incomplete.
scoring_rubric.3: 50-70% of critical equipment records have accurate nameplate data; specifications available for some.
scoring_rubric.4: 70-90% of critical equipment records are complete and accurate; verified through field audits within the last 2 years.
scoring_rubric.5: >90% of all equipment records are complete, accurate, and field-verified; ongoing data stewardship process maintains quality.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor who field-verifies 10 critical pump records and finds 8 have accurate manufacturer, model, serial number, and specification data scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are labor hours accurately recorded on work orders to enable meaningful cost analysis and future job plan estimates?
questions.evidence_guidance: Compare CMMS labor hours against time-keeping or payroll records for a sample week. Assess variance and identify causes of inaccuracy.

scoring_rubric.1: Labor hours are not recorded on work orders or are wildly inaccurate.
scoring_rubric.2: Labor hours are recorded but in round estimates (e.g., all 8-hour entries); accuracy within 50%.
scoring_rubric.3: Labor hours are recorded with reasonable accuracy (within 30%); some technicians are more accurate than others.
scoring_rubric.4: Labor hours are recorded accurately (within 15%) by task or work order; used for cost analysis and future planning estimates.
scoring_rubric.5: Labor hours tracked to task level with start/stop times via mobile CMMS; variance analysis between planned and actual informs continuous improvement.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 85% of work orders have labor hours recorded within 15% of actual (verified against timekeeping) and planners use historical data for future estimates scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a process for maintaining data quality when new equipment is installed, existing equipment is modified, or equipment is decommissioned?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the process for adding new equipment to CMMS and decommissioning old equipment. Check recent capital project handover records for CMMS data completeness.

scoring_rubric.1: No process for updating CMMS when equipment changes occur; data becomes stale over time.
scoring_rubric.2: Equipment additions/changes are sometimes reflected in CMMS but the process is inconsistent and delayed.
scoring_rubric.3: A process exists requiring project teams to provide CMMS data for new equipment; compliance is variable.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal process with defined data requirements for new equipment, modification documentation, and decommissioning; project handover checklist includes CMMS updates.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive asset data lifecycle management; project close-out requires verified CMMS data; MOC process triggers data updates; ongoing data stewardship ensures continuous accuracy.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where every capital project has a CMMS data checklist that must be completed before the asset is accepted into the maintenance program, including hierarchy, nameplate, BOMs, and PM schedules, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are regular data quality audits conducted on CMMS data with results reported and corrective actions tracked?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request data quality audit reports from the last 12 months. Check for defined audit criteria, findings, and corrective action tracking.

scoring_rubric.1: No data quality audits have been conducted.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional ad-hoc checks on data quality; no formal audit process.
scoring_rubric.3: Periodic data quality audits (semi-annual or annual) with basic metrics.
scoring_rubric.4: Quarterly data quality audits covering key fields; results reported to management; corrective actions tracked to closure.
scoring_rubric.5: Continuous data quality monitoring with automated dashboards; monthly audit reports; data quality KPIs included in management reviews; dedicated data stewardship role.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site conducting quarterly CMMS data audits that check 5 key fields across 50 work orders, reports results to the maintenance manager, and tracks corrective actions scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are standardized naming conventions and coding schemes enforced in the CMMS to enable consistent data analysis across the organization?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review CMMS naming conventions for equipment, locations, and failure codes. Check for a documented standard and assess consistency in a sample of records.

scoring_rubric.1: No naming conventions exist; equipment names are inconsistent and non-standard.
scoring_rubric.2: Some conventions exist but are not enforced; inconsistency across areas.
scoring_rubric.3: Documented naming conventions; generally followed but legacy inconsistencies remain.
scoring_rubric.4: Enforced naming conventions with documented standards; CMMS configuration restricts non-standard entries where possible; legacy data being cleaned up.
scoring_rubric.5: Rigorous naming standards aligned with industry taxonomy (e.g., ISO 14224); fully enforced; automated validation prevents non-standard entries.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a documented equipment naming standard (Area-Equipment Type-Sequential Number) that is enforced for all new records and applied during a legacy data cleanup project scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a clear data ownership model defining who is responsible for the accuracy of different categories of CMMS data?

scoring_rubric.1: No data ownership defined; nobody is accountable for data quality.
scoring_rubric.2: IT owns the system; no maintenance data ownership defined.
scoring_rubric.3: Informal understanding that planners own work order data; other data categories unassigned.
scoring_rubric.4: Documented data ownership model: planners own work order data, reliability owns failure data, engineering owns asset master data; accountability enforced.
scoring_rubric.5: Formal data governance structure with data stewards for each category, defined quality standards, escalation procedures, and executive sponsorship.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with documented data ownership where the reliability engineer owns failure code accuracy, the planner owns work order data quality, and the engineering team owns asset master data scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Can maintenance cost data (labor, materials, contractor) be accurately extracted from the CMMS and allocated to individual assets for lifecycle cost analysis?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request a maintenance cost report for the top 10 highest-cost assets. Verify that labor, materials, and contractor costs are captured and allocated accurately.

scoring_rubric.1: Maintenance costs cannot be extracted by asset; costs are tracked only at the department level.
scoring_rubric.2: Some cost data available by area but not by individual asset; material costs often not linked to work orders.
scoring_rubric.3: Cost data available by asset for labor and materials; contractor costs may not be fully allocated; accuracy is approximate.
scoring_rubric.4: Accurate maintenance cost tracking by asset for labor, materials, and contractors; enables bad-actor and lifecycle cost analysis.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive cost tracking with cost-per-unit-of-production analysis, TCO calculations, and cost data integrated into repair-vs-replace decisions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that can produce an accurate 12-month maintenance cost report for any critical asset — showing labor, parts, and contractor spend — and uses it for bad-actor prioritization scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a process for ensuring that CMMS data entry is timely — work orders created, updated, and closed within defined timeframes?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review average time from work completion to WO closure in CMMS. Check for aging open work orders that should have been closed.

scoring_rubric.1: Data entry is chronically delayed; work orders are closed weeks or months after completion or not at all.
scoring_rubric.2: Significant delays in data entry; many work orders closed more than 2 weeks after completion.
scoring_rubric.3: Most work orders closed within 1-2 weeks; some lag especially during busy periods.
scoring_rubric.4: Work orders closed within 3-5 business days of completion; timeliness expectations are defined and monitored.
scoring_rubric.5: Real-time data entry via mobile CMMS; work orders closed same day or next day; timeliness tracked as a KPI.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where 85% of work orders are closed within 5 business days of completion and timeliness is reported to supervisors weekly scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the organization's use of maintenance and reliability data analytics to drive improvement decisions and resource allocation.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review reliability analysis reports, bad-actor lists, and examples of data-driven decisions. Interview managers about how they use data for resource allocation.

scoring_rubric.1: No reliability data analysis occurs; decisions are based on instinct and experience.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic reporting (WO counts, costs) exists but is descriptive only; no analytical depth.
scoring_rubric.3: Some failure analysis performed (Pareto, top-10 bad actors); results shared in periodic reviews.
scoring_rubric.4: Regular reliability analysis including failure patterns, cost trends, MTBF/MTTR, and bad-actor prioritization; results drive PM optimization and capital investment decisions.
scoring_rubric.5: Advanced analytics including predictive modeling, survival analysis, and AI-assisted pattern recognition; data analytics is a strategic capability integrated with asset management planning.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a monthly bad-actor report using Pareto analysis, MTBF trending for critical assets, and evidence that 3 PM strategy changes were data-driven in the last year scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is a formal bad-actor or chronic equipment failure elimination program in place with regular reviews and tracked improvement actions?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the bad-actor list and program governance. Check for selection criteria, assigned owners, action plans, and demonstrated results.

scoring_rubric.1: No bad-actor program; chronic failures persist unaddressed.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal awareness of bad actors; no structured program for elimination.
scoring_rubric.3: Bad-actor list exists and is reviewed periodically; some investigations occur.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal bad-actor program with data-driven selection, assigned owners, RCA for each, tracked action plans, and demonstrated failure reduction.
scoring_rubric.5: Mature bad-actor elimination program integrated with capital planning; chronic failures systematically eliminated; program has produced measurable OEE improvement.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a monthly top-10 bad-actor review, each with an assigned owner and RCA, where 6 out of 10 bad actors from last year have been resolved scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are key reliability metrics (MTBF, MTTR, OEE/availability, failure rates) calculated and trended for critical equipment?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request MTBF, MTTR, and availability trend charts for the top 10 critical assets. Verify data source and calculation methodology.

scoring_rubric.1: No reliability metrics are calculated.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic availability data exists (uptime/downtime) but no MTBF, MTTR, or failure rate analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: MTBF and/or MTTR calculated for some critical equipment; trending is limited.
scoring_rubric.4: MTBF, MTTR, and availability trended monthly for all critical assets; metrics reviewed in reliability meetings and drive improvement priorities.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive reliability metrics with Weibull analysis, failure distribution modeling, and integration with lifecycle cost analysis; metrics drive capital and operational decisions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with monthly MTBF and MTTR trend charts for all Criticality A equipment, reviewed in the bi-weekly reliability meeting and used to prioritize improvement efforts, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are root cause analysis (RCA) investigations conducted for significant equipment failures using a structured methodology?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 3-5 RCA reports from the last 12 months. Assess methodology, team composition, root cause depth, corrective actions, and action tracking.

scoring_rubric.1: No formal RCA is conducted for equipment failures.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional informal investigation of major failures; no structured methodology.
scoring_rubric.3: RCA conducted for major failures using a basic methodology (5-Why); reports are generated but action follow-through is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Structured RCA program with defined triggers, trained facilitators, cross-functional teams, and tracked corrective actions; 80%+ action completion rate.
scoring_rubric.5: Mature RCA program using advanced methods (fault tree, PROACT, TapRoot); proactive RCA for near-misses; institutional learning shared across sites; all actions closed within deadlines.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with defined RCA triggers (>$10K cost or safety impact), trained facilitators using 5-Why/fishbone methodology, cross-functional teams, and 85% action completion rate scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is maintenance cost data analyzed relative to asset replacement value (maintenance cost as % of RAV) to benchmark spending effectiveness?
questions.evidence_guidance: Determine total annual maintenance cost and estimated replacement asset value (RAV). Calculate the ratio and compare to industry benchmarks.

scoring_rubric.1: Maintenance cost as % of RAV is not calculated or RAV is unknown.
scoring_rubric.2: Total maintenance cost is known but RAV is estimated; ratio calculated but not benchmarked.
scoring_rubric.3: Maintenance cost as % of RAV is calculated and compared to industry benchmarks; ratio is in the upper quartile.
scoring_rubric.4: Maintenance cost as % of RAV is tracked annually, benchmarked against industry peers, and within the target range (2-4% for most industries); trends improving.
scoring_rubric.5: Maintenance cost analysis includes total cost of ownership, maintenance cost per unit of production, and asset-level cost benchmarking; cost optimization is data-driven.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that calculates annual maintenance cost as 3.2% of RAV, compares it to the industry benchmark of 2.5-4%, and tracks a downward trend over 3 years scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization use data-driven repair-vs-replace analysis to make capital investment decisions for aging or chronic-failure assets?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 2-3 examples of repair-vs-replace analyses conducted in the last 2 years. Check for maintenance cost trends, failure rates, and lifecycle cost calculations.

scoring_rubric.1: No repair-vs-replace analysis is performed; assets are replaced only when catastrophic failure occurs.
scoring_rubric.2: Replacement decisions are based on opinion and urgency; no data analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: Some repair-vs-replace analysis based on maintenance cost data; methodology is informal.
scoring_rubric.4: Structured repair-vs-replace analysis using maintenance costs, failure rates, and downtime impact; results feed the capital planning process.
scoring_rubric.5: Lifecycle cost modeling integrates acquisition, maintenance, downtime, energy, and end-of-life costs; predictive analytics inform optimal replacement timing.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where a 5-year maintenance cost trend and increasing failure rate for a cooling water pump justified capital replacement, and the analysis was documented in the CAPEX request, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are PdM condition monitoring trends analyzed to optimize monitoring intervals and validate alert thresholds?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review PdM trending practices. Check for examples of interval adjustment or threshold optimization based on data analysis.

scoring_rubric.1: PdM data is not trended; readings are point-in-time only.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic trending exists but intervals and thresholds are vendor defaults; no optimization.
scoring_rubric.3: Trends reviewed periodically; some interval adjustments based on equipment condition.
scoring_rubric.4: Systematic trending with optimized intervals based on P-F curve analysis; alert thresholds validated against actual failure data.
scoring_rubric.5: Advanced PdM analytics with machine learning, automated trend detection, dynamic interval adjustment, and integration with digital twin models.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that has adjusted vibration monitoring intervals from monthly to weekly on deteriorating assets and validated alarm thresholds by correlating PdM data with actual failure events scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is downtime data systematically categorized (planned vs. unplanned, maintenance vs. operations vs. external) to enable accurate loss analysis?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review downtime tracking methodology, categorization scheme, and loss analysis reports. Verify that maintenance-caused downtime can be accurately isolated.

scoring_rubric.1: Downtime is not tracked or is tracked only as a total number without categorization.
scoring_rubric.2: Downtime is tracked but categorization is vague; planned and unplanned are not separated.
scoring_rubric.3: Downtime categorized as planned/unplanned and by department; some loss analysis possible.
scoring_rubric.4: Detailed downtime categorization (planned, unplanned, scheduled, forced, external) with asset-level attribution; loss analysis identifies top downtime drivers.
scoring_rubric.5: Real-time downtime tracking integrated with process historian; automated categorization; financial impact calculated per event; dashboards show loss waterfalls.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with downtime categorized as scheduled, forced-maintenance, forced-operations, and external, tracked at the equipment level, with a monthly Pareto of top 10 downtime drivers, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are spare parts inventory levels optimized based on criticality, consumption data, and lead time analysis rather than arbitrary stocking decisions?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review spare parts stocking methodology. Check for min/max calculations, criticality-based stocking policies, and inventory turnover metrics.

scoring_rubric.1: Spare parts stocking is ad-hoc; no analysis of consumption or lead times.
scoring_rubric.2: Min/max levels exist for some items but are based on historical practice rather than analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: Min/max levels set for high-value and critical spares based on consumption data; periodic review occurs.
scoring_rubric.4: Inventory optimized using criticality, consumption rates, lead times, and cost; safety stock calculations applied; inventory turnover tracked.
scoring_rubric.5: Advanced inventory optimization with probabilistic models, dynamic reorder points, vendor-managed inventory for commodities, and total inventory investment benchmarking.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with min/max levels calculated from 24-month consumption data and supplier lead times for all critical spares, reviewed annually, with inventory turnover tracked as a KPI, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization use leading indicators (PdM findings, defect backlog, PM compliance, near-miss reports) in addition to lagging indicators (failures, costs, downtime) to manage reliability performance?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the maintenance KPI dashboard. Identify which metrics are leading vs. lagging. Check whether leading indicators are reviewed and acted upon.

scoring_rubric.1: Only lagging indicators (failures, costs, downtime) are tracked; no leading indicators.
scoring_rubric.2: PM compliance tracked as a leading indicator; other leading indicators not used.
scoring_rubric.3: Several leading indicators tracked (PM compliance, backlog health, PdM findings) but not consistently reviewed for action.
scoring_rubric.4: Balanced scorecard of leading and lagging indicators; leading indicators reviewed weekly and trigger proactive actions.
scoring_rubric.5: Predictive KPI framework with leading indicators driving resource allocation and management attention before failures occur; statistical process control applied to key metrics.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a KPI dashboard showing both leading indicators (PM compliance, schedule compliance, defect backlog age) and lagging indicators (MTBF, downtime, cost), reviewed weekly, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the maturity of the organization's asset management policy and strategic asset management plan (SAMP) in guiding maintenance and reliability decisions.
questions.evidence_guidance: Request the asset management policy and SAMP documents. Check for alignment with ISO 55001, leadership endorsement, and communication to the workforce.

scoring_rubric.1: No asset management policy or strategic plan exists.
scoring_rubric.2: A maintenance policy exists but is not aligned with asset management principles; no SAMP.
scoring_rubric.3: Asset management policy drafted; SAMP exists in basic form; alignment with ISO 55001 is partial.
scoring_rubric.4: Asset management policy endorsed by leadership; SAMP aligns with business objectives; communicated to the organization; reviewed annually.
scoring_rubric.5: ISO 55001-aligned policy and SAMP; fully integrated with business strategy; drives all asset investment and maintenance decisions; certified or audit-ready.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a signed asset management policy, a SAMP that defines 5-year objectives aligned with production targets, and annual review evidence scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are long-term asset lifecycle plans established for critical assets covering maintenance strategy, capital renewal, and end-of-life decisions?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review lifecycle plans for 3-5 critical asset classes. Check for condition assessment, maintenance strategy, capital renewal schedule, and end-of-life criteria.

scoring_rubric.1: No lifecycle planning; assets are operated until they fail catastrophically.
scoring_rubric.2: Some awareness of aging assets; no documented lifecycle plans.
scoring_rubric.3: Basic lifecycle plans for a few critical asset classes; focused primarily on replacement timing.
scoring_rubric.4: Lifecycle plans for all critical asset classes covering condition, maintenance strategy, renewal investment, and disposal; updated based on condition assessment data.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive lifecycle management integrated with financial planning; risk-based investment optimization; predictive models inform optimal intervention timing.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with 10-year lifecycle plans for major equipment classes (e.g., transformers, compressors, heat exchangers) that include condition grades, maintenance strategies, and projected replacement dates scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal risk management framework that includes equipment failure risks alongside safety, environmental, and production risks?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the site risk register. Check for equipment failure risks, their consequence ratings, and associated mitigation actions.

scoring_rubric.1: No risk management framework or equipment failure risks are not considered.
scoring_rubric.2: General safety risk register exists but equipment reliability risks are not included.
scoring_rubric.3: Equipment failure risks are included in the risk register for critical assets; mitigation actions are basic.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal risk framework with equipment failure risks rated by consequence and likelihood; mitigation actions link to maintenance strategies and capital investments.
scoring_rubric.5: Integrated risk management with quantitative risk analysis, bow-tie methodology for critical assets, risk-based maintenance optimization, and risk appetite defined by leadership.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a risk register that includes the top 20 equipment failure scenarios, each with consequence ratings, likelihood assessments, and linked mitigation plans, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are asset management objectives cascaded from strategic goals to operational targets with clear linkage between business outcomes and maintenance activities?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the objective cascade from business plan to maintenance targets. Check for line-of-sight between strategic goals and frontline KPIs.

scoring_rubric.1: No linkage between business objectives and maintenance activities.
scoring_rubric.2: Maintenance has its own internal objectives but they are not linked to business strategy.
scoring_rubric.3: Some linkage exists (e.g., production targets drive PM priorities) but it is not formalized.
scoring_rubric.4: Asset management objectives are cascaded from business strategy through maintenance targets to frontline KPIs; personnel understand how their work contributes.
scoring_rubric.5: Full line-of-sight from corporate strategy through SAMP to maintenance objectives, KPIs, and individual performance goals; strategy deployment methodology (e.g., Hoshin Kanri) in use.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where the business goal of 95% production availability is cascaded to a maintenance availability target, PM compliance target, and technician-level wrench time goal scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a defined and funded capital renewal program for major equipment based on condition assessment, age, and reliability data?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the capital renewal/replacement plan and budget. Check for condition-based prioritization, multi-year funding, and alignment with asset lifecycle plans.

scoring_rubric.1: No capital renewal program; all capital spending is reactive (replace after failure).
scoring_rubric.2: Some capital requests for equipment replacement; ad-hoc and not programmatic.
scoring_rubric.3: Annual capital budget includes equipment renewal; prioritization is based on urgency and departmental advocacy.
scoring_rubric.4: Multi-year capital renewal program based on asset condition assessments, reliability data, and risk-based prioritization; funded annually.
scoring_rubric.5: Optimized capital planning with risk-cost models, condition-based investment timing, integration with SAMP, and long-term funding commitments.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 5-year capital renewal plan based on condition grades for major equipment, annually funded at $2M, and prioritized using a risk-based scoring methodology scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are regulatory and compliance requirements for equipment inspection, testing, and maintenance systematically identified and managed?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the regulatory compliance register for maintenance-related requirements (pressure vessels, electrical, environmental, safety). Check compliance tracking and audit results.

scoring_rubric.1: Regulatory requirements for equipment are not identified or managed systematically.
scoring_rubric.2: Some awareness of regulatory requirements; compliance is managed by individual departments without central coordination.
scoring_rubric.3: Regulatory requirements identified; compliance tracked for most items but gaps exist; some overdue inspections.
scoring_rubric.4: Comprehensive regulatory compliance register; all requirements tracked in CMMS with automated scheduling; compliance rate >95%; audit-ready documentation.
scoring_rubric.5: Full regulatory compliance with proactive engagement; zero overdue regulatory inspections; compliance integrated into asset management system; regular internal audits verify compliance.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a regulatory compliance register listing all OSHA, EPA, and state-mandated inspections, tracked in CMMS with 97% on-time completion, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is the maintenance budget developed with input from reliability data, backlog analysis, and asset condition assessments rather than being purely incremental or top-down?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the maintenance budget development process. Check for inputs from CMMS data, backlog analysis, condition assessments, and reliability improvement priorities.

scoring_rubric.1: Budget is set by finance/management with no maintenance input; purely top-down.
scoring_rubric.2: Budget is incremental (last year +/- %); maintenance provides minimal input.
scoring_rubric.3: Budget considers backlog size and known upcoming work; some reliability data informs major line items.
scoring_rubric.4: Zero-based or needs-based budgeting with inputs from CMMS data, backlog analysis, asset condition, and planned reliability improvements.
scoring_rubric.5: Strategic budgeting integrating lifecycle cost analysis, risk-based investment decisions, and predictive models; budget directly linked to asset management objectives and KPI targets.

questions.calibration_anchor: A maintenance manager who builds the annual budget using CMMS cost trends, backlog labor hours, planned PM/PdM program costs, and a prioritized list of reliability improvements scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization benchmark its maintenance and reliability practices and performance against industry peers or recognized standards?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review benchmarking activities, industry survey participation, or peer comparison data.

scoring_rubric.1: No benchmarking occurs; the organization operates in isolation.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal awareness of industry practices through conferences or publications; no formal benchmarking.
scoring_rubric.3: Participates in occasional industry surveys; some metric comparison available.
scoring_rubric.4: Regular benchmarking against industry peers using recognized surveys; gap analysis drives improvement priorities.
scoring_rubric.5: Systematic benchmarking program with internal cross-site and external peer comparisons; best practice identification; active participation in industry benchmarking consortia.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that participates in an annual industry benchmarking survey, compares key metrics (cost/RAV, PM compliance, planned %), and uses gaps to set improvement targets scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance and reliability considered during the design and procurement phase of new equipment (design for maintainability/reliability)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review capital project procedures for maintenance/reliability input requirements. Check recent project examples for evidence of design review participation.

scoring_rubric.1: Maintenance is not consulted during equipment design or procurement; 'thrown over the wall' to maintain.
scoring_rubric.2: Maintenance occasionally provides informal input on major projects; no formal design review process.
scoring_rubric.3: Maintenance and reliability review is required for major capital projects; input is sometimes incorporated.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal design review process includes maintenance/reliability at key stages (conceptual, detailed, FAT); life-cycle cost analysis informs procurement decisions.
scoring_rubric.5: Maintenance and reliability are embedded in the capital project process; RAM analysis, FMEA, and LCC are standard deliverables; design for maintainability standards exist.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where the reliability engineer participates in design reviews for all capital projects >$100K, provides input on maintainability, and influences equipment selection decisions scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a documented asset management system (AMS) that defines the processes, roles, tools, and governance for managing physical assets across their lifecycle?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request documentation of the asset management system. Check for process maps, role definitions, tool specifications, and governance structure.

scoring_rubric.1: No documented asset management system exists.
scoring_rubric.2: Some maintenance processes are documented but not integrated into an asset management system.
scoring_rubric.3: Key asset management processes are documented; some integration between maintenance, engineering, and operations processes.
scoring_rubric.4: Documented AMS covering asset lifecycle from acquisition through disposal; defined processes, roles, and governance; aligned with ISO 55001.
scoring_rubric.5: Mature AMS certified to ISO 55001; comprehensive process documentation, continuous improvement, internal audit program, and management review.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a documented asset management system that includes process maps for work management, asset lifecycle planning, and performance monitoring — aligned with ISO 55001 clause structure — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the maturity of the maintenance and reliability KPI system — are the right metrics measured, trended, reviewed, and acted upon?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the maintenance KPI dashboard or scorecard. Assess balance of leading/lagging metrics, trending, review cadence, and evidence of action taken on metric deviations.

scoring_rubric.1: No maintenance KPIs are measured or reported.
scoring_rubric.2: A few basic metrics tracked (WO count, costs) but not regularly reviewed or acted upon.
scoring_rubric.3: Standard KPIs tracked (PM compliance, backlog, costs, availability); reviewed monthly; some actions taken on significant deviations.
scoring_rubric.4: Comprehensive KPI system with leading and lagging indicators, weekly/monthly reviews at multiple levels, defined targets, and accountability for corrective actions.
scoring_rubric.5: World-class performance management with tiered review cadence, statistical process control, predictive metrics, benchmarking, and direct linkage between KPI performance and business outcomes.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with 12-15 maintenance KPIs including both leading and lagging indicators, reviewed weekly by supervisors and monthly by management, with defined targets and action plans for gaps, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance KPI targets established based on data analysis, benchmarking, and business objectives rather than arbitrary numbers?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review how KPI targets were set. Check for benchmarking data, historical trend analysis, or business objective linkage.

scoring_rubric.1: No KPI targets exist.
scoring_rubric.2: Targets are set arbitrarily (e.g., 'improve 10%') without analytical basis.
scoring_rubric.3: Some targets based on historical performance trends; others are aspirational.
scoring_rubric.4: Targets derived from benchmarking, statistical analysis of historical performance, and alignment with business objectives; stretch targets are realistic.
scoring_rubric.5: Targets are optimized using statistical methods, aligned with industry best practice, cascaded from strategic objectives, and updated annually based on performance and changing conditions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where PM compliance target of 90% was set based on current performance (82%), industry benchmark (92%), and a phased improvement plan scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a tiered performance review cadence (daily/weekly/monthly) with appropriate metrics and participants at each level?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the meeting cadence and agenda for daily, weekly, and monthly reviews. Check who attends and what metrics are discussed.

scoring_rubric.1: No regular performance reviews occur.
scoring_rubric.2: Monthly management review only; no frontline review cadence.
scoring_rubric.3: Weekly supervisor-level review and monthly management review; metrics are discussed but actions are informal.
scoring_rubric.4: Tiered cadence: daily (supervisor — work status), weekly (area — KPIs and actions), monthly (management — trends and strategy); formal action tracking at each level.
scoring_rubric.5: Integrated tiered management system with visual boards, leader standard work, escalation protocols, and seamless flow of information from floor to executive level.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a daily morning meeting (supervisor/crew), weekly reliability review (area managers), and monthly management review (plant leadership) — each with defined metrics and action logs — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) or a similar composite metric calculated and used to drive improvement on critical production equipment?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review OEE calculations for critical equipment. Check methodology, data sources, and how OEE results drive improvement actions.

scoring_rubric.1: OEE is not calculated or the concept is not understood.
scoring_rubric.2: OEE is calculated for some equipment but methodology is inconsistent or data quality is suspect.
scoring_rubric.3: OEE calculated consistently for critical equipment; results shared but improvement actions are informal.
scoring_rubric.4: OEE tracked for all critical equipment with accurate data; loss categorization (availability, performance, quality) enables targeted improvement; results reviewed in management meetings.
scoring_rubric.5: OEE is a primary business metric with real-time tracking, automated data collection, six big loss analysis, and structured improvement teams driving OEE gains.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site calculating OEE monthly for 10 critical lines with breakdown into availability, performance, and quality losses, and using the data to prioritize improvement projects, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance and reliability KPIs visible and accessible to frontline maintenance personnel, not just management?
questions.evidence_guidance: Walk through maintenance shop and control rooms. Look for visual performance boards, digital displays, or accessible dashboards showing maintenance KPIs.

scoring_rubric.1: No maintenance KPIs are visible to frontline personnel.
scoring_rubric.2: KPIs exist in management reports but are not communicated to the frontline.
scoring_rubric.3: Some KPIs posted in the maintenance shop but are outdated or not discussed.
scoring_rubric.4: Current KPIs displayed on visual boards or digital displays in work areas; discussed in daily/weekly team meetings; personnel understand the metrics.
scoring_rubric.5: Interactive KPI dashboards accessible to all; real-time performance visibility; team-level metrics drive daily decisions; personnel take ownership of their metrics.

questions.calibration_anchor: An auditor finding a current maintenance KPI board in the shop with PM compliance, schedule compliance, and safety metrics — and technicians can explain the trends — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is individual and team performance in maintenance linked to reliability KPI outcomes through performance management or incentive programs?

scoring_rubric.1: No link between individual/team performance and reliability outcomes.
scoring_rubric.2: General performance reviews exist but maintenance/reliability KPIs are not included.
scoring_rubric.3: Some reliability metrics included in supervisor/manager performance objectives.
scoring_rubric.4: Reliability KPIs are part of performance evaluations for supervisors and managers; team-level incentives tied to key metrics (PM compliance, availability).
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive performance alignment from technician to plant manager; reliability behaviors and outcomes are recognized and rewarded; balanced scorecard approach prevents gaming.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where maintenance supervisor performance reviews include PM compliance, rework rate, and schedule compliance targets — with clear consequences for sustained underperformance — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance audit or assessment programs conducted periodically to identify gaps and drive continuous improvement?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review audit/assessment history, including this RMI audit. Check for previous assessments, gap closure tracking, and reassessment schedules.

scoring_rubric.1: No maintenance assessments or audits have been conducted.
scoring_rubric.2: One-time assessment completed at some point; recommendations largely unaddressed.
scoring_rubric.3: Periodic assessments (every 2-3 years); recommendations tracked with partial implementation.
scoring_rubric.4: Regular assessments (annual or biennial) with structured gap closure tracking; reassessment validates improvement.
scoring_rubric.5: Continuous assessment program with self-assessment, peer review, and external validation; integrated with management review process; assessment drives strategic planning.
