questions.calibration_anchor: A site conducting biennial RMI assessments with a structured action plan, quarterly progress reviews, and demonstrated improvement in scores between assessments scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance cost metrics tracked and analyzed at a level that enables meaningful cost optimization (cost per asset, cost per unit of production, labor vs. materials breakdown)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review maintenance cost reports. Check granularity (department, area, asset level), breakdown (labor, materials, contractor), and comparison to budget.

scoring_rubric.1: Maintenance cost is tracked only as a total department budget number.
scoring_rubric.2: Costs tracked by area or cost center; no asset-level cost visibility.
scoring_rubric.3: Costs tracked by area and major equipment; labor/materials/contractor breakdown available; compared to budget monthly.
scoring_rubric.4: Cost tracked at asset level with labor/material/contractor breakdown; cost per unit of production calculated; cost trends analyzed for optimization opportunities.
scoring_rubric.5: Activity-based costing with full cost allocation; TCO analysis for major assets; cost optimization integrated with reliability strategy; zero-based budgeting approach.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that tracks maintenance cost per ton of production, can break down costs by asset with labor/material/contractor split, and uses cost trends to identify optimization opportunities scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are safety leading indicators (near-misses, hazard reports, safety observations) from maintenance activities tracked and trended separately from the overall site safety metrics?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review safety metrics for the maintenance department specifically. Check for trending of maintenance-specific leading indicators.

scoring_rubric.1: No maintenance-specific safety metrics exist.
scoring_rubric.2: Maintenance injury rates tracked as part of overall site metrics; no maintenance-specific leading indicators.
scoring_rubric.3: Maintenance injury rates tracked separately; some leading indicators (near-misses) reported.
scoring_rubric.4: Maintenance-specific safety dashboard with lagging (injuries, DART) and leading (near-misses, hazard reports, observations) indicators; trends reviewed monthly.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive maintenance safety metrics integrated with reliability data; equipment-related safety risks linked to maintenance strategy; proactive safety culture indicators tracked.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a monthly maintenance safety dashboard showing TRIR, near-miss reports, LOTO compliance audit results, and safety observation counts — reviewed in the management meeting — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does management review of maintenance performance include trend analysis and forward-looking risk assessment, not just snapshot reporting of current metrics?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the most recent monthly management review presentation. Check for trend charts, predictive analysis, and risk assessment sections.

scoring_rubric.1: No management review of maintenance performance occurs.
scoring_rubric.2: Management receives snapshot reports of current metrics; no trend analysis.
scoring_rubric.3: Monthly reports include some trend charts; primarily backward-looking.
scoring_rubric.4: Management review includes 12-month trend analysis, forward-looking risk assessment (aging asset risks, workforce risks, backlog trends), and improvement projections.
scoring_rubric.5: Strategic management review with predictive analytics, scenario modeling, risk-adjusted performance forecasts, and direct linkage to strategic asset management decisions.

questions.calibration_anchor: A monthly management review that includes 12-month KPI trends, a 'watch list' of deteriorating assets, workforce retirement risk, and projected backlog growth under current resources scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Rate the organization's commitment to and execution of continuous improvement in maintenance and reliability practices.
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the reliability improvement roadmap or plan. Check for active improvement projects, resource allocation, and demonstrated results over the last 2 years.

scoring_rubric.1: No continuous improvement activity in maintenance; the status quo is accepted.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional improvement projects driven by crises; no systematic approach.
scoring_rubric.3: Some improvement initiatives underway; a reliability improvement plan exists but execution is sporadic.
scoring_rubric.4: Active continuous improvement program with a prioritized roadmap, dedicated resources, defined methodology, and measurable results.
scoring_rubric.5: Continuous improvement is embedded in culture; structured methodology (Lean, Six Sigma, RCM); employee-driven improvement suggestions; rapid implementation; demonstrated year-over-year improvement.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 2-year reliability improvement roadmap, 5 active improvement projects with assigned owners, and demonstrated improvements in 3 KPIs over the last year scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a formal process for identifying, prioritizing, and implementing reliability improvement projects with ROI tracking?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review the improvement project selection process. Check for prioritization criteria, project charters, progress tracking, and ROI calculation for completed projects.

scoring_rubric.1: No formal process for improvement project identification or management.
scoring_rubric.2: Improvement ideas collected informally; implementation depends on individual initiative.
scoring_rubric.3: Improvement projects are identified and some have informal tracking; ROI is not measured.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal project selection with risk/ROI prioritization; projects managed with charters, milestones, and progress reviews; ROI calculated for completed projects.
scoring_rubric.5: Portfolio management approach to improvement projects; stage-gate process; resource leveling; comprehensive benefits tracking; improvement investment yields measurable business returns.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a quarterly improvement project review where each project has a charter, an ROI estimate, assigned resources, and milestone tracking — and 3 completed projects showed positive ROI last year — scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are frontline maintenance personnel actively engaged in identifying and implementing improvement ideas, not just management-driven initiatives?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review improvement suggestion volumes from frontline staff. Interview technicians about their ability to propose and implement improvements. Check for a suggestion system.

scoring_rubric.1: Frontline personnel are not engaged in improvement; 'just do your job' mentality.
scoring_rubric.2: Some technicians informally suggest improvements; no formal mechanism to capture or act on ideas.
scoring_rubric.3: Suggestion system exists; some ideas implemented but feedback to submitters is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Active frontline engagement; suggestion system with rapid evaluation and feedback; technicians participate in improvement teams; ideas implemented regularly.
scoring_rubric.5: Frontline-driven improvement culture; high suggestion volume with rapid implementation; technicians lead Kaizen events; continuous improvement is recognized and rewarded.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where technicians submit an average of 2 improvement suggestions per month through a formal system, 60% are implemented within 90 days, and submitters receive feedback scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are structured problem-solving methodologies (5-Why, A3, DMAIC, Kaizen) used consistently for maintenance and reliability improvement initiatives?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 3-5 recent improvement projects or RCAs. Check for evidence of structured methodology application.

scoring_rubric.1: No structured problem-solving methodologies used; improvement efforts are unstructured.
scoring_rubric.2: Basic 5-Why used occasionally; no formal methodology training.
scoring_rubric.3: Structured methodology used for major issues; some personnel trained; application is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Standard problem-solving methodology adopted; personnel trained; methodology applied consistently for RCAs and improvement projects.
scoring_rubric.5: Multiple methodologies mastered (5-Why, A3, DMAIC, Kaizen); widely used at all levels; internal trainers and facilitators; methodology discipline is a cultural norm.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site where all RCAs use 5-Why methodology, improvement projects use A3 problem-solving, and 10+ personnel have been trained in the methodologies scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization conduct post-implementation reviews for completed improvement projects to verify sustained results and capture lessons learned?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review 3 completed improvement projects for evidence of post-implementation review, sustained results verification, and documented lessons learned.

scoring_rubric.1: No post-implementation reviews; projects are considered 'done' once implemented.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional informal review of major projects; no systematic process.
scoring_rubric.3: Post-implementation reviews occur for some projects; sustainability check is basic.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal 90-day post-implementation review for all significant projects; results validated against targets; lessons learned documented and shared.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive sustainability audits at 30/90/180 days; control plans ensure results are maintained; lessons learned feed future project planning; documented in knowledge base.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that conducts a 90-day review for every completed improvement project, verifies KPI improvement was sustained, and documents 3 lessons learned per project scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is there a reliability improvement roadmap or master plan that provides a multi-year vision for advancing maintenance and reliability maturity?
questions.evidence_guidance: Request the reliability improvement roadmap. Check for phased objectives, resource requirements, timeline, and linkage to business strategy.

scoring_rubric.1: No reliability improvement roadmap exists.
scoring_rubric.2: Informal goals exist but no documented roadmap or master plan.
scoring_rubric.3: A 1-year improvement plan exists with some defined initiatives.
scoring_rubric.4: Multi-year (2-5 year) reliability improvement roadmap with phased objectives, defined resources, milestones, and alignment with business strategy.
scoring_rubric.5: Comprehensive transformation roadmap integrated with SAMP; phased implementation with maturity targets; governance structure drives execution; annually updated.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site with a 3-year reliability roadmap showing Year 1 (foundational — CMMS data quality, PM optimization), Year 2 (growth — PdM expansion, RCM), and Year 3 (advanced — analytics, IoT), with resources allocated, scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Are maintenance process audits conducted regularly to verify adherence to defined work management processes (planning, scheduling, execution, closeout)?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review process audit schedules, checklists, and findings. Check for corrective actions taken when non-compliance is identified.

scoring_rubric.1: No maintenance process audits are conducted.
scoring_rubric.2: Occasional reviews of work management processes; not structured or scheduled.
scoring_rubric.3: Annual process audits with checklists; findings documented but follow-up is inconsistent.
scoring_rubric.4: Quarterly process audits covering planning, scheduling, execution, and closeout; findings tracked to corrective action closure; audit results trended.
scoring_rubric.5: Continuous process verification through layered audits, supervisor standard work, and automated compliance monitoring; process adherence is a cultural expectation.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site conducting quarterly work management process audits with a standard checklist, documenting findings, and tracking corrective actions to closure within 60 days scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization learn from industry events, incidents at other facilities, and emerging best practices to proactively improve its maintenance and reliability programs?

scoring_rubric.1: No engagement with industry events or external learning.
scoring_rubric.2: Some personnel attend conferences; no structured mechanism to apply learnings.
scoring_rubric.3: Industry alerts and peer incidents are circulated; some best practices adopted informally.
scoring_rubric.4: Structured process for reviewing industry incidents, alerts, and best practices; relevant items actioned; personnel participate in industry forums and professional associations.
scoring_rubric.5: Active industry leadership through committee participation, paper presentations, and peer networking; systematic adoption of emerging best practices; internal innovation program.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that reviews industry incident alerts monthly, checks applicability to their operations, and has adopted 3 best practices from industry engagement in the past year scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Is change management applied when implementing significant maintenance process improvements to ensure adoption and sustainability?
questions.evidence_guidance: Review a recent significant process change (e.g., new scheduling process, CMMS upgrade). Check for change management elements: communication, training, stakeholder engagement, adoption measurement.

scoring_rubric.1: No change management; changes are mandated without communication, training, or support.
scoring_rubric.2: Changes are communicated but training and support are minimal; adoption struggles are common.
scoring_rubric.3: Basic change management with communication and training; adoption monitoring is informal.
scoring_rubric.4: Structured change management methodology applied to significant changes; includes stakeholder analysis, communication plan, training, adoption metrics, and sustainment plan.
scoring_rubric.5: Change management is a core competency; dedicated change management resources; every significant change follows a proven methodology; adoption rates tracked; resistance proactively managed.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that implemented a new weekly scheduling process using a structured change plan with stakeholder engagement, phased rollout, training, and 90-day adoption tracking scores a 4.

questions.question_text: Does the organization have a mechanism to prioritize and balance competing improvement initiatives to avoid initiative overload and ensure focus?

scoring_rubric.1: No prioritization of improvement initiatives; multiple competing programs create confusion and fatigue.
scoring_rubric.2: Many initiatives launched but not prioritized; resources spread thin; completion rates are low.
scoring_rubric.3: Some prioritization occurs but initiative overload is still a problem; sequencing is informal.
scoring_rubric.4: Formal prioritization process with defined criteria (ROI, strategic alignment, risk, resources); active initiatives limited to manageable number; resource loading considered.
scoring_rubric.5: Portfolio management of improvement initiatives with strategic alignment, resource optimization, stage-gate governance, and executive steering; organization executes with focus and discipline.

questions.calibration_anchor: A site that limits active reliability improvement projects to 5 at any time, selects them based on ROI and strategic alignment, and completes 80% on schedule scores a 4.
